```mermaid
    C2 --> C6[Schedule III Handling Rules]
    C2 --> C7[Schedule IV Specific Waste Types]
    C2 --> C8[CPCB Central Pollution Control Board]
    C8 --> C9[SPCB State Pollution Control Board]
    C9 --> C10[Industry Operator Authorization]
    C10 --> C11[Annual Returns Submission]
    C10 --> C12[Site Inspection and Audit]
    C11 --> C13[Compliance Verification]
    C12 --> C13
    C13 --> C14{Compliant}
    C14 -- YES --> C15[Operation Continued]
    C14 -- NO --> C16[Closure Notice and Penalty]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define hazardous waste as per the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), 1976. Mention any four industries that generate hazardous waste.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** As per RCRA 1976, hazardous waste is any solid, liquid, or contained gaseous waste that, due to its **quantity, concentration, physical, chemical, or infectious characteristics**, may cause or significantly contribute to an increase in mortality or pose substantial present or potential hazard to human health or the environment when improperly treated, stored, transported, or disposed of.
>
> **Four Industries (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Petroleum Refineries** — spent catalysts, slop oil, BTEX-contaminated wastewater.
> 2. **Pharmaceutical Industry** — active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) waste, fermentation residues, antibiotic-contaminated solvents.
> 3. **Metal Plating Industry** — cyanide baths, chromic acid solutions, cadmium-bearing rinse water.
> 4. **Pesticide Manufacturing Industry** — obsolete pesticides, organochlorine residues, container washings.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the four characteristics used by the US-EPA to classify hazardous waste under RCRA. Write the threshold limit for any two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> The four RCRA characteristics are commonly remembered as **"ICRT"** (1 Mark):
> 1. **I — Ignitability (D001)**
> 2. **C — Corrosivity (D002)**
> 3. **R — Reactivity (D003)**
> 4. **T — Toxicity (D004–D043)**
>
> **Threshold Limits (2 Marks — 1 each):**
> - **Ignitability:** Flash point **< 60 °C (140 °F)** for liquid wastes.
> - **Corrosivity:** Aqueous pH **≤ 2.0** (strong acid) or **≥ 12.5** (strong base); or corrodes SAE 1020 steel at **> 6.35 mm/year** at 55 °C.
> - *Acceptable alternative for second mark:* **Toxicity** — leachate concentration via TCLP exceeding regulatory limits for 40 listed contaminants (e.g., Lead: **5.0 mg/L**).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE)

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure (TCLP)** in detail. Mention its significance, extraction fluid selection criteria, and list any six toxic constituents tested along with their regulatory limits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Indian regulatory framework for hazardous waste management** with reference to the **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management & Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016**. Outline the roles of CPCB and SPCB.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) TCLP — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** TCLP is a laboratory extraction procedure developed by the US-EPA (Method 1311, SW-846) to simulate the leaching behaviour of contaminants from solid waste under **co-disposal landfill conditions**. It determines whether a waste is classified as **toxic (D004–D043)** under RCRA.

> **Procedure Steps (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Sample Preparation:** Waste is size-reduced to particles < 9.5 mm.
> 2. **Weighing:** 100 grams of sample is taken.
> 3. **Extraction Fluid Selection (1 Mark — Significance):**
>    - **Fluid #1:** Sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.93 ± 0.05) — used when waste alkalinity is low or for general cases.
>    - **Fluid #2:** Acetic acid buffer (pH 2.88 ± 0.05) — used when waste contains **sulphides, paints, or other reactive alkalies**.
> 4. **Extraction:** Sample is placed in a rotary extractor with 2 L of extraction fluid at **30 rpm** for **18 hours** at **23 ± 2 °C**.
> 5. **Filtration:** Leachate is filtered through a **0.7 micron glass fibre filter**.
> 6. **Analysis:** Leachate is acidified with HNO₃ to pH < 2 and analysed using **ICP-AES (Inductively Coupled Plasma – Atomic Emission Spectroscopy)** or **AAS (Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy)**.

> **Six Toxic Constituents with Regulatory Limits (3 Marks — 0.5 each):**

| Contaminant | CAS No. | TCLP Limit (mg/L) |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| **Lead (Pb)** | 7439-92-1 | 5.0 |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | 7440-43-9 | 1.0 |
| **Mercury (Hg)** | 7439-97-6 | 0.2 |
| **Arsenic (As)** | 7440-38-2 | 5.0 |
| **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** | 18540-29-9 | 5.0 |
| **Benzene** | 71-43-2 | 0.5 |

---

**(b) Indian Regulatory Framework (7 Marks):**

> **Origin (1 Mark):** The Indian hazardous waste rules operate under the umbrella of the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. The current governing regulation is the **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management & Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016**, which replaced the 2008 rules.

> **Key Provisions of the 2016 Rules (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Schedule I — Process-Specific Wastes:** Lists 47 industrial processes (e.g., petroleum refining, chrome plating, pesticides, pharmaceutical manufacture) and assigns waste codes.
> 2. **Schedule II — Waste Constituents:** Defines concentration thresholds for 17 hazardous constituents (heavy metals, cyanides, phenols, etc.).
> 3. **Schedule III — Handling, Storage, and Packaging:** Mandates proper labelling with **Hazardous Waste Label**, container compatibility, and storage period (≤ 90 days).
> 4. **Schedule IV — Specific Waste Types:** Special handling for **used lead-acid batteries, e-waste, bio-medical waste, and waste oils**.
> 5. **Transboundary Movement:** Requires **prior informed consent (PIC)** under the **Basel Convention** for importing/exporting hazardous waste.
> 6. **Responsibilities:** Every "occupier" generating hazardous waste must obtain **authorization from SPCB** and submit **annual returns** in **Form 4**.

> **Roles of CPCB and SPCB (2 Marks):**
> - **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board):** Apex body that lays down **uniform standards**, conducts **national-level monitoring**, and advises the Central Government on policy. It maintains the **National Inventory of Hazardous Waste** and coordinates transboundary movement.
> - **SPCB (State Pollution Control Board):** State-level authority that **grants authorization** to hazardous waste generators, **inspects facilities**, **enforces compliance**, and forwards consolidated data to CPCB.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **classification of hazardous waste** based on the **RCRA four-characteristic system** with at least two examples for each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **working of a rotary kiln hazardous waste incinerator**. Calculate the Destruction and Removal Efficiency (DRE) for a waste stream fed at **500 kg/hr** with stack emission of **0.02 kg/hr**, and check compliance with EPA standards.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) RCRA Four-Characteristic Classification (7 Marks):**

> **Introduction (1 Mark):** The US-EPA under **40 CFR Part 261.20–261.24** classifies hazardous waste through four characteristic-based codes (**D001–D043**) in addition to listed wastes.

> **Detailed Classification (4 Marks — 1 each):**

> **1. Ignitability (D001):**
> - **Criteria:** Flash point < 60 °C (140 °F); or is an oxidizer per DOT regulations; or is an ignitable compressed gas.
> - **Examples:** Spent acetone from paint shops, used petroleum-based solvents, gasoline-contaminated absorbents, ammonium perchlorate.

> **2. Corrosivity (D002):**
> - **Criteria:** Aqueous pH ≤ 2 or ≥ 12.5; or corrodes SAE 1020 steel at > 6.35 mm/yr at 55 °C.
> - **Examples:** Spent sulphuric acid from lead-acid battery manufacturing, spent caustic from refineries, pickling acid (HCl) from steel plants.

> **3. Reactivity (D003):**
> - **Criteria:** Unstable, reacts violently with water, generates toxic gases, explosive under normal conditions, or cyanide/sulphide-bearing wastes that release H₂S or HCN.
> - **Examples:** Sodium metal, picric acid (if dry), sodium azide, sodium hypochlorite mixed with ammonia.

> **4. Toxicity (D004–D043):**
> - **Criteria:** TCLP leachate concentration exceeds limits for any of 40 listed contaminants (heavy metals, pesticides, PCBs).
> - **Examples:** D008 (Lead > 5.0 mg/L in leachate), D010 (Selenium > 1.0 mg/L), D018 (Benzene > 0.5 mg/L), D035 (Methyl ethyl ketone > 200 mg/L).

> **Listed Wastes Supplement (2 Marks):**
> - **F-list** (Wastes from Non-Specific Sources): F001 (spent halogenated solvents), F003 (spent non-halogenated solvents like xylene, acetone).
> - **K-list** (Wastes from Specific Industries): K048 (dissolved air flotation float from petroleum refining), K061 (emission control dust from primary steel smelting).
> - **P-list** (Acutely Hazardous Commercial Chemical Products): P001 (warfarin > 0.3%), P030 (cyanides).
> - **U-list** (Toxic Commercial Chemical Products): U001 (acetaldehyde), U154 (methanol).

---

**(b) Rotary Kiln Hazardous Waste Incinerator (7 Marks):**

> **Description (3 Marks):** A rotary kiln incinerator consists of a **slightly inclined rotating cylindrical chamber** (length 10–40 m, diameter 1–5 m) lined with **refractory bricks**. Waste is fed at the upper end and slowly tumbles as the kiln rotates at **0.5–5 rpm**. The residence time is typically **30–90 minutes**, with operating temperatures of **850–1100 °C** in the kiln and **1100–1300 °C** in the secondary combustion chamber (afterburner).

> **Working Steps (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Waste Feed:** Solid/liquid/sludge hazardous waste is injected through a feed system at the kiln inlet.
> 2. **Primary Combustion:** Kiln rotates; tumbling action dries and volatilizes organics, with partial combustion.
> 3. **Secondary Combustion:** Combustion gases enter the **afterburner** with additional air for complete oxidation (> 2 seconds at > 1100 °C).
> 4. **Air Pollution Control (APC):** Flue gas passes through **quench, scrubber, baghouse/ESP** before release through stack.
> 5. **Ash Handling:** Bottom ash (clinker) and fly ash are quenched and sent to **secure landfill**.

> **DRE Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$DRE = \frac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100$$

$$DRE = \frac{500 - 0.02}{500} \times 100$$

$$DRE = \frac{499.98}{500} \times 100$$

$$DRE = 0.99996 \times 100 = 99.996\%$$

> **Compliance Check (1 Mark — 0.5 marks):**
> $$DRE_{achieved} = 99.996\% \; > \; DRE_{EPA \, standard} = 99.99\%$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The incinerator **COMPLIES** with the EPA standard for principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs).

---

## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hazardous Waste Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Listed" vs. "Characteristic" wastes:** Students often write only one category. Both must be explained for full marks (each carries 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting units in TCLP / DRE:** Writing "5.0" instead of "5.0 mg/L" loses 0.5 marks. Always specify units.
> 3. **Mixing up threshold pH values:** Saying "pH < 7" instead of "pH ≤ 2 or ≥ 12.5" is a generic error. Use the **exact regulatory values**.
> 4. **Skipping CPCB vs. SPCB distinction:** Examiners specifically test this. CPCB is policy-making; SPCB is implementation.
> 5. **Not mentioning the 2016 Rules update:** If the question says "Indian framework," always cite the **2016 Rules** (not the 1989 or 2008 versions).
> 6. **Wrong equation in DRE:** Writing DRE = $W_{out} / W_{in}$ instead of $(W_{in} - W_{out}) / W_{in}$ loses 2 full marks.
> 7. **Ignoring the 18-hour, 30 rpm parameters** in TCLP — these are frequently tested as "fill-in-the-blank" items.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield "Must-Remember" Points for Hazardous Wastes:**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** HW = waste that threatens health/environment due to quantity, concentration, or characteristic (RCRA 1976).
> - ✅ **"ICRT" Mnemonic:** Ignitability (D001), Corrosivity (D002), Reactivity (D003), Toxicity (D004–D043).
> - ✅ **Flash Point Threshold:** < 60 °C for ignitability.
> - ✅ **Corrosivity pH:** ≤ 2.0 (acid) or ≥ 12.5 (base); steel corrosion > 6.35 mm/yr.
> - ✅ **TCLP Method 1311:** 100 g sample + 2 L extraction fluid + 18 hours at 23 °C + 30 rpm + 0.7 µm filter.
> - ✅ **TCLP Lead Limit:** 5.0 mg/L; **Mercury:** 0.2 mg/L; **Cadmium:** 1.0 mg/L; **Benzene:** 0.5 mg/L.
> - ✅ **EPA DRE Standard:** ≥ 99.99% for principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs).
> - ✅ **Incineration:** Rotary kiln primary + afterburner secondary ≥ 1100 °C for ≥ 2 sec residence.
> - ✅ **Indian Rules:** Hazardous and Other Wastes Rules 2016 (4 Schedules: I, II, III, IV).
> - ✅ **CPCB:** Apex national body (policy + standards); **SPCB:** State-level authorization + enforcement.
> - ✅ **Schedule I:** 47 processes; **Schedule II:** 17 constituents; **Schedule III:** Handling/storage rules; **Schedule IV:** Special wastes (lead-acid batteries, e-waste, bio-medical).
> - ✅ **Basel Convention:** Governs transboundary movement of hazardous waste; requires Prior Informed Consent (PIC).
> - ✅ **Treatment Hierarchy:** Source reduction → Segregation → Treatment (chemical/physical/biological) → Secure landfill.
> - ✅ **Stabilization/Solidification:** Cement-based for inorganic HW (heavy metals).
> - ✅ **Indian Generation Rate:** ~ 7.5 million tonnes/year (CPCB 2023 inventory).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Solid Wastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Solid Wastes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Waste (KTU 2024 Definition):** Any discarded or abandoned material, substance, or object arising from human activities, which is solid, semi-solid, liquid, or contained gaseous material, and is managed through integrated solid waste management (ISWM) principles under the **Solid Waste Management Rules (SWM), 2016** of India.

The **Properties of Solid Wastes** are the quantitative and qualitative physico-chemical-biological characteristics that determine how a given waste stream will behave during **collection, storage, transportation, treatment, and final disposal**. According to the KTU PECHT861 syllabus (Module 1), the properties are classified into three primary domains:

1. **Physical Properties** — describe *what the waste looks like and how it behaves mechanically*.
2. **Chemical Properties** — describe *what the waste is made of and how it reacts*.
3. **Biological Properties** — describe *how the waste interacts with living systems and decomposes*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Curry" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of municipal solid waste as a **leftover curry in your kitchen**.
> - **Moisture Content** = how watery/soupy the curry is.
> - **Density** = how heavy the container feels.
> - **Calorific Value** = how much heat it gives when you (hypothetically) burn it.
> - **C/N Ratio** = the spice-to-base ratio — too spicy (high N) and microbes choke; too bland (high C) and they starve.
> - **Particle Size** = whether it's chunky (vegetable peels) or fine (tea dust).
>
> Just as a chef needs to know these properties before recycling leftovers, a waste engineer must characterize waste before designing a treatment plant.

---

## 1.3 The Three-Domain Classification (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Property Hierarchy:**
> 
> | Domain | Key Parameters |
> |---|---|
> | **Physical** | Moisture Content, Density (Bulk & Particle), Particle Size & Distribution, Porosity, Permeability, Field Capacity |
> | **Chemical** | Proximate & Ultimate Analysis, Calorific Value, Fixed Carbon, Volatile Matter, Ash, pH, C/N Ratio, Fats/Oils/Greases |
> | **Biological** | Biodegradability, Respirometry (BOD/COD for leachate), Microbial Activity |

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants

The following constants and standards are universally used in SWM engineering:

- **Moisture Content Standard Reference:** Oven dried at **103 – 105 °C** for 24 hours (per IS/ASTM D2216 convention).
- **Calorific Value Reference:** Gross Calorific Value (GCV) measured in a **Bomb Calorimeter**, units **kJ/kg** or **kcal/kg** (**1 kcal = 4.186 kJ**).
- **Density of Water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 4 °C.
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** 0 °C and 101.325 kPa for gas volume measurements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Properties — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Moisture Content (MC)

**Definition:** The weight of water per unit weight of wet waste, expressed as a percentage.

**Why it matters:** High MC reduces calorific value, increases transport cost, and complicates incineration.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Weigh the wet waste sample → $W_{wet}$
2. Oven-dry at 103–105 °C for 24 hours until constant mass.
3. Weigh the dried sample → $W_{dry}$
4. The water lost = $W_{wet} - W_{dry}$

### 2.1.2 Density

- **Bulk Density ($D_b$):** Mass per unit volume *as-collected*, including voids. Used for designing collection vehicles and landfill capacity.
- **Particle Density ($D_p$):** Mass per unit volume *excluding voids*. Used for compaction modeling.
- **Field Capacity:** Maximum water content (by weight) that a waste mass can retain against gravity — critical for landfill leachate estimation.

### 2.1.3 Particle Size & Distribution

Waste particles range from sub-millimeter (dust) to bulky items (>100 mm). Distribution is typically characterized by **sieve analysis**, with results plotted as a semi-log grading curve.

### 2.1.4 Porosity & Permeability

- **Porosity ($n$):** Void volume / Total volume.
- **Permeability ($k$):** Capacity of waste mass to transmit fluids (Darcy's Law governs leachate flow).

---

## 2.2 Chemical Properties — Logic Flow

### 2.2.1 Proximate Analysis

Determines four fractions:
- **Moisture (M)** — water content (already discussed).
- **Volatile Matter (VM)** — gases released when heated to 950 °C in absence of air (combustibles).
- **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — solid combustible residue after VM release.
- **Ash (A)** — non-combustible inorganic residue.

**Mass Balance:**

$$\text{M + VM + FC + A} = 100\%$$

### 2.2.2 Ultimate Analysis

Determines elemental composition: **C, H, O, N, S, Ash** (in % by dry weight). Used for stoichiometric combustion calculations.

### 2.2.3 Calorific Value

- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV) / Higher Heating Value (HHV):** Heat released with water from combustion condensed.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV) / Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Heat released with water as vapor (the *useful* heat in real incinerators).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** All industrial incinerator designs use **NCV** for sizing the combustion chamber. GCV is a laboratory property.

### 2.2.4 pH and C/N Ratio

- **pH:** Indicates acidity/alkalinity of waste and leachate. Affects microbial activity.
- **C/N Ratio (Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio):** Governs the rate of biological decomposition. **Ideal for aerobic composting = 25 : 1 to 35 : 1**.

---

## 2.3 Biological Properties

- **Biodegradability Index:** Fraction of organic matter decomposable by microbes.
- **BOD₅ / COD of Leachate:** Indicates strength of liquid pollution from waste.
- **Methane Yield:** Reported as m³ CH₄ per kg of VS (volatile solids) destroyed — critical for landfill gas design (typically **0.4 – 0.5 m³/kg VS**).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Master Formula Table (Print and Memorize)**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $MC_{dry} = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Bulk Density | $D_b = \dfrac{M}{V_{bulk}}$ | kg/m³ |
| 4 | Particle Density | $D_p = \dfrac{M}{V_{particle}}$ | kg/m³ |
| 5 | Porosity | $n = 1 - \dfrac{D_b}{D_p}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Volatile Solids Fraction | $VS = \dfrac{M_{dry} - M_{ash}}{M_{dry}}$ | fraction |
| 7 | Calorific Value (Dulong's Eqn) | $GCV = 337C + 1418\Big(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\Big) + 93S$ | kJ/kg |
| 8 | Energy Recovery from MSW | $E = m \times NCV \times \eta$ | kJ |
| 9 | Moisture to NCV correction | $NCV = GCV - h_{fg}\Big(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\Big)$ | kJ/kg |
| 10 | C/N Ratio | $CN = \dfrac{\%C_{organic}}{\%N_{total}}$ | ratio |
| 11 | Methane Generation (Scholl Canyon) | $Q = k \cdot L_0 \cdot M \cdot e^{-kt}$ | m³/yr |

**Key Constants:**
- Latent heat of vaporization of water: $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg
- Dulong's equation: valid for high carbon fuels; H, O, S in % dry mass.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These properties are used for:
- **Vehicle sizing:** $V_{truck} = \dfrac{\text{Daily waste (kg)}}{D_b}$ → decides number of collection trips.
- **Landfill volume estimation:** $V_{landfill} = \dfrac{M_{total}}{D_{compacted}}$
- **Incinerator sizing:** $E_{thermal} = \dot{m} \times NCV$ → decides grate area.
- **Compost stability:** C/N ratio determines if compost is mature.
- **Leachate treatment design:** BOD/COD load governs plant capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Moisture Content (Wet Basis)

**Starting Concept:** A sample of fresh municipal solid waste is collected in a tray. The tray mass is $T$ kg, and the sample with the tray is $W_1$ kg. After oven-drying at 105 °C for 24 hours, the weight becomes $W_2$ kg.

**Step 1 — Establish weights:**

$$W_{wet} = W_1 - T$$

$$W_{dry} = W_2 - T$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water evaporated:**

$$M_{water} = W_{wet} - W_{dry} = (W_1 - T) - (W_2 - T)$$

Simplifying the bracket expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{water} &= W_1 - T - W_2 + T \\
M_{water} &= W_1 - W_2
\end{aligned}$$

This is logical: the tray mass **cancels out**.

**Step 3 — Define Moisture Content on Wet Basis:**

$$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{water}}{W_{wet}} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - T}$$

**Step 4 — Express as percentage:**

$$MC_{wb}\% = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - T} \times 100$$

**Engineering Insight:** Wet basis MC is always **less than or equal to 100%**, while dry basis MC can exceed 100% (e.g., sludges).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Dulong's Equation for Calorific Value

Dulong's empirical equation relates the gross calorific value of a fuel to its elemental composition.

**Starting Formula:**

$$GCV = 337C + 1418\Big(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\Big) + 93S$$

**Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step:**

- **Term 1: $337C$** — Each kg of carbon releases 33,700 kJ on combustion: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 33700 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Dividing by 100 (since C is in %) gives $337C$.

- **Term 2: $1418(H - O/8)$** — Hydrogen burns to water. However, if oxygen is already present (as in MSW), part of the H₂ is already "pre-oxidized" as moisture. The oxygen consumes $O/8$ fraction of hydrogen stoichiometrically (since $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$). Net combustible hydrogen = $H - O/8$, each kg yielding 141,800 kJ → coefficient 1418.

- **Term 3: $93S$** — Sulphur burns to $SO_2$ releasing 9,300 kJ/kg → coefficient 93.

**Final expression in kJ/kg (with C, H, O, S in dry mass %):**

$$\boxed{GCV = 337C + 1418\Big(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\Big) + 93S \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Net Calorific Value from Gross CV

**Step 1:** GCV includes the latent heat of water vapor condensation. In real incinerators, flue gas leaves hot → water exits as vapor.

**Step 2:** Total water produced per kg of fuel:
- From fuel moisture $M$ (% by mass).
- From hydrogen combustion: each kg of H produces 9 kg of water → $\dfrac{9H}{100}$ kg of water per kg fuel.

$$m_{water} = \dfrac{9H + M}{100} \quad \text{[kg water per kg fuel]}$$

**Step 3:** Energy lost as latent heat:

$$Q_{latent} = h_{fg} \cdot m_{water} = 2260 \times \dfrac{9H + M}{100}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract from GCV:

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - Q_{latent} \\
NCV &= GCV - h_{fg}\Big(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\Big)
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example:** A waste with GCV = 12,000 kJ/kg, H = 5%, M = 30%:

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 12000 - 2260 \times \dfrac{9(5) + 30}{100} \\
&= 12000 - 2260 \times \dfrac{45 + 30}{100} \\
&= 12000 - 2260 \times 0.75 \\
&= 12000 - 1695 \\
&= 10305 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **15% drop** in usable energy due to moisture — exactly why Indian MSW (40–60% MC) is poor for incineration without pre-drying.

---

## 3.4 Porosity Derivation from Densities

**Concept:** Porosity is the void fraction in a waste mass.

$$n = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}}$$

The volume of solids per unit total volume is:

$$\dfrac{V_{solids}}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{M/D_p}{M/D_b} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_p}$$

Since $V_{solids} + V_{voids} = V_{total}$, dividing by $V_{total}$:

$$\dfrac{D_b}{D_p} + n = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{n = 1 - \dfrac{D_b}{D_p}}$$

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Matrix — KTU Style Problems

### Problem 1 — Moisture Content

A waste sample weighing **2.450 kg** (with tray of 0.350 kg) is oven-dried. The dry sample + tray weighs **1.150 kg**. Calculate **wet basis MC** and **dry basis MC**.

**Solution:**

$$W_{wet} = 2.450 - 0.350 = 2.100 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{dry} = 1.150 - 0.350 = 0.800 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_{water} = 2.100 - 0.800 = 1.300 \text{ kg}$$

$$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{1.300}{2.100} \times 100 = 61.90\%$$

$$MC_{db} = \dfrac{1.300}{0.800} \times 100 = 162.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Indian MSW typically has 40–65% MC (wet basis). A dry-basis MC > 100% indicates very wet waste like food/yard waste.

### Problem 2 — Dulong's Calorific Value

A solid waste has ultimate analysis (dry): C = 40%, H = 6%, O = 30%, S = 1%, Ash = 23%. Estimate GCV.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 337(40) + 1418\Big(6 - \dfrac{30}{8}\Big) + 93(1) \\
&= 13480 + 1418(6 - 3.75) + 93 \\
&= 13480 + 1418(2.25) + 93 \\
&= 13480 + 3190.5 + 93 \\
&= 16763.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Check:** A well-segregated MSW should have GCV in the range **8000 – 18000 kJ/kg**. Our value of ~16763 kJ/kg indicates *energy-rich* waste (e.g., plastics + paper rich).

### Problem 3 — Landfill Volume Estimation

A city generates **500 tonnes/day** of MSW with bulk density **300 kg/m³**. It is compacted to **700 kg/m³** at the landfill. Calculate (a) daily volume in collection trucks, (b) daily landfill volume.

**Solution:**

**(a) As-collected (loose):**

$$V_{loose} = \dfrac{500{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{300 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1666.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(b) Compacted at landfill:**

$$V_{compact} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{700} = 714.29 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Volume reduction ratio:**

$$\dfrac{V_{loose}}{V_{compact}} = \dfrac{1666.67}{714.29} \approx 2.33$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Note:** A 2.33× volume reduction means a 1-hectare landfill cell of 10 m depth serves approximately:
>
> $$\dfrac{10000 \times 10}{714.29} = 140 \text{ days} \approx 4.5 \text{ months}$$
>
> This drives the **landfill lifespan estimation** — a key KTU problem.

### Problem 4 — C/N Ratio and Compost Stability

A composting mix has 30 kg carbon (organic) and 1.2 kg nitrogen.

$$CN = \dfrac{30}{1.2} = 25$$

This is **optimal** for rapid composting — no amendment needed.

If $CN = 50$ (typical for fresh leaves), add nitrogen-rich material (e.g., sewage sludge with $CN = 8$).

Using mass balance:

$$C_{mix} = 30 + 8x, \quad N_{mix} = 1.2 + x$$

$$CN_{target} = 25 \Rightarrow \dfrac{30 + 8x}{1.2 + x} = 25$$

$$\begin{aligned}
30 + 8x &= 25(1.2 + x) \\
30 + 8x &= 30 + 25x \\
8x - 25x &= 30 - 30 \\
-17x &= 0 \\
x &= 0 \quad \text{(paradox — needs correction)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Correction:** Actually if leaves CN = 50 means 50 kg C per 1 kg N. For 30 kg C: N = 30/50 = 0.6 kg. Then:

$$\dfrac{30 + 8x}{0.6 + x} = 25$$

$$\begin{aligned}
30 + 8x &= 25(0.6 + x) \\
30 + 8x &= 15 + 25x \\
30 - 15 &= 25x - 8x \\
15 &= 17x \\
x &= 0.882 \text{ kg sludge}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lesson:** Always verify the C/N of the *base* material before applying mass balance — a common KTU valuation trap.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Solid Waste Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SOLID WASTE PROPERTIES"] --> B["PHYSICAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> C["CHEMICAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> D["BIOLOGICAL PROPERTIES"]

    B --> B1["Moisture Content"]
    B --> B2["Bulk Density"]
    B --> B3["Particle Density"]
    B --> B4["Particle Size"]
    B --> B5["Porosity"]
    B --> B6["Permeability"]
    B --> B7["Field Capacity"]

    C --> C1["Proximate Analysis"]
    C --> C2["Ultimate Analysis"]
    C --> C3["Calorific Value"]
    C --> C4["pH"]
    C --> C5["C to N Ratio"]

    C1 --> C1a["Moisture"]
    C1 --> C1b["Volatile Matter"]
    C1 --> C1c["Fixed Carbon"]
    C1 --> C1d["Ash"]

    C2 --> C2a["Carbon"]
    C2 --> C2b["Hydrogen"]
    C2 --> C2c["Oxygen"]
    C2 --> C2d["Nitrogen"]
    C2 --> C2e["Sulphur"]

    D --> D1["Biodegradability"]
    D --> D2["BOD and COD of Leachate"]
    D --> D3["Methane Yield"]
    D --> D4["Microbial Activity Index"]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: How Waste Properties Drive Engineering Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Moisture Content"] --> D1["Incinerator Drying Zone"]
    P2["Calorific Value"] --> D2["Combustion Chamber Sizing"]
    P3["Bulk Density"] --> D3["Collection Vehicle Capacity"]
    P4["C to N Ratio"] --> D4["Compost Pad Design"]
    P5["Porosity"] --> D5["Landfill Leachate Collection"]
    P6["Biodegradability"] --> D6["Anaerobic Digester Sizing"]
    P7["Particle Size"] --> D7["Shredder and Screen Selection"]

    D1 --> OUT["INTEGRATED SWM PLANT"]
    D2 --> OUT
    D3 --> OUT
    D4 --> OUT
    D5 --> OUT
    D6 --> OUT
    D7 --> OUT
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Workflow: Lab Determination of Moisture Content

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Collect Fresh Waste Sample"] --> B["Weigh Wet Sample plus Tray"]
    B --> C["Oven Dry at 103 to 105 C for 24 Hours"]
    C --> D{"Constant Mass Achieved?"}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E["Weigh Dried Sample plus Tray"]
    E --> F["Subtract Tray Mass"]
    F --> G["Compute Moisture Content Percent"]
    G --> H["Report Wet Basis or Dry Basis"]
```

---

## 4.4 Process Flow: Proximate vs Ultimate Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Raw Solid Waste Sample"] --> B["Air Dry and Grind"]
    B --> C1["Proximate Analysis Track"]
    B --> C2["Ultimate Analysis Track"]

    C1 --> P1["105 C Oven: Moisture"]
    C1 --> P2["950 C Inert Atm: Volatile Matter"]
    C1 --> P3["750 C Air: Ash and Fixed Carbon"]

    C2 --> U1["CHNS Analyzer"]
    C2 --> U2["Oxygen by Difference"]
    C2 --> U3["Ash from Furnace"]

    P1 --> OUT1["Proximate Composition Report"]
    P2 --> OUT1
    P3 --> OUT1

    U1 --> OUT2["Ultimate Composition Report"]
    U2 --> OUT2
    U3 --> OUT2
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Energy Recovery Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Determine Waste Calorific Value"] --> B{"NCV Greater Than 1200 kcal per kg?"}
    B -- No --> X1["Skip Incineration: Use Landfill or Composting"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Check Moisture Content"]
    C --> D{"Moisture Less Than 50 Percent?"}
    D -- No --> Y1["Pre-Drying Required: Use Mechanical or Thermal Dryer"]
    D -- Yes --> E["Proceed to Incineration with Energy Recovery"]
    E --> F["Boiler and Steam Turbine"]
    F --> G["Electricity Export to Grid"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Moisture Content of solid waste. Why is it considered the most important physical property?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Moisture Content is the ratio of the mass of water present in a waste sample to the total mass of the wet sample, expressed as a percentage.
>
> **Formula:** $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$
>
> **Why most important (any 3 points):**
> 1. **Affects calorific value:** High moisture reduces NCV drastically (each 10% rise reduces NCV by ~10–15%).
> 2. **Affects transport cost:** Wet waste is heavier per unit dry mass.
> 3. **Affects decomposition rate:** Anaerobic digestion slows at high MC; composting requires MC ≈ 50–60%.
> 4. **Affects leachate generation in landfills:** Field capacity is governed by MC.
> 5. **Decides pre-treatment selection:** If MC > 50%, mechanical or thermal drying is needed before incineration.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between Bulk Density and Particle Density of solid waste.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bulk Density | Particle Density |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Mass per unit volume *as collected*, including voids | Mass per unit volume of *individual particles*, excluding voids |
| **Includes voids?** | Yes | No |
| **Typical MSW Value** | 100 – 300 kg/m³ (loose); 500 – 700 kg/m³ (compacted) | 800 – 1500 kg/m³ |
| **Used for** | Vehicle design, landfill volume, storage bin design | Compaction modeling, porosity calculation |
| **Determined by** | Weighing a known container volume | Pycnometer or water displacement method |

**Formula relating them:** $n = 1 - \dfrac{D_b}{D_p}$ where $n$ is porosity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the physical properties of municipal solid waste in detail. Discuss how each property influences the design of a sanitary landfill. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following ultimate analysis data is given for a solid waste sample (dry basis):
> C = 38%, H = 5%, O = 28%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 27.3%.
> If the moisture content is 30%, determine:
> (i) Gross Calorific Value using Dulong's equation
> (ii) Net Calorific Value
> (iii) Comment on the suitability for incineration. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Physical Properties and Landfill Design

**1. Moisture Content:**
- Definition and formula (already discussed).
- **Landfill impact:** High MC generates more leachate; reduces stability of waste mass; field capacity must be estimated.

**2. Bulk Density:**
- Determines compaction ratio and landfill volume.
- **Landfill impact:** Compaction with bulldozers increases density from ~200 to ~700 kg/m³, reducing airspace consumption.

**3. Particle Size & Distribution:**
- Affects compaction, void ratio, and leachate flow paths.
- **Landfill impact:** Pre-shredding reduces large voids, improves cover material contact.

**4. Porosity & Permeability:**
- High porosity = high void space = more landfill gas storage, more leachate drainage capacity.
- **Landfill impact:** Determines leachate collection pipe spacing (Darcy's Law used).

**5. Field Capacity:**
- Maximum water waste can hold against gravity.
- **Landfill impact:** Leachate generation starts only after field capacity is exceeded.

**6. Compressibility:**
- Waste settles under its own weight.
- **Landfill impact:** Settlement allowance (typically 10–20% of fill height) must be included in design.

**Concluding Statement:**
"Landfill design integrates all these physical properties — bulk density for capacity, permeability for drainage, compressibility for settlement, and field capacity for leachate estimation."

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Calorific Value Calculation

**Given:** C = 38%, H = 5%, O = 28%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.5%, M = 30%

**(i) Gross Calorific Value (Dulong's Equation):**

$$GCV = 337C + 1418\Big(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\Big) + 93S$$

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 337(38) + 1418\Big(5 - \dfrac{28}{8}\Big) + 93(0.5) \\
&= 12806 + 1418(5 - 3.5) + 46.5 \\
&= 12806 + 1418(1.5) + 46.5 \\
&= 12806 + 2127 + 46.5 \\
&= 14979.5 \text{ kJ/kg (dry basis)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convert to as-received basis (include moisture 30%):**

$$GCV_{ar} = GCV_{dry} \times (1 - M/100) = 14979.5 \times 0.70 = 10485.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Net Calorific Value:**

Using $NCV = GCV - h_{fg}\Big(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\Big)$ (on dry basis first):

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV_{dry} &= 14979.5 - 2260 \times \dfrac{9(5) + 0}{100} \\
&= 14979.5 - 2260 \times 0.45 \\
&= 14979.5 - 1017 \\
&= 13962.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convert to as-received basis (M = 30):**

$$NCV_{ar} = 13962.5 \times (1 - 0.30) - 2260 \times \dfrac{M}{100}$$

**More accurate approach (directly):**

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV_{ar} &= GCV_{ar} - h_{fg}\Big(\dfrac{9H_{dry} \cdot (1-M) + M \cdot 100}{100}\Big) \\
&= 10485.65 - 2260 \times \dfrac{9 \times 5 \times 0.70 + 30}{100} \\
&= 10485.65 - 2260 \times \dfrac{31.5 + 30}{100} \\
&= 10485.65 - 2260 \times 0.615 \\
&= 10485.65 - 1389.9 \\
&= 9095.75 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Suitability for Incineration:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold for incineration without auxiliary fuel: NCV > ~5000 kJ/kg (~1200 kcal/kg).**
>
> Our $NCV_{ar} = 9095.75$ kJ/kg = **2173 kcal/kg** → **YES, suitable for incineration with energy recovery.**
>
> Note: The waste has reasonably good energy content, but the 30% moisture has reduced the available energy by ~30%. Pre-drying would improve efficiency further.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the procedure for the determination of **proximate analysis** of solid waste. List the typical composition of Indian municipal solid waste. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A landfill receives 800 tonnes/day of MSW with an average loose bulk density of 250 kg/m³. After compaction in the landfill cell, the density increases to 750 kg/m³. The landfill site has an area of 10 hectares and a planned depth of 15 m. Determine:
> (i) The annual volume occupied by the compacted waste.
> (ii) The expected life of the landfill in years.
> (iii) The compaction ratio achieved. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Proximate Analysis

**Flowchart:**

```
Sample → Air Drying → Pulverization → 4 Parallel Tracks:
   Track 1: 105 °C Oven (24 h) → Moisture (M)
   Track 2: 950 °C Muffle Furnace (7 min, N₂ atm) → Volatile Matter (VM)
   Track 3: 750 °C Muffle Furnace (with O₂) → Ash (A)
   Track 4: Fixed Carbon (FC) = 100 - (M + VM + A)
```

**Steps:**
1. Sample collected, air-dried, weighed, and ground to < 0.2 mm.
2. **Moisture:** 1 g sample → 105 °C oven → 24 h → reweigh.
3. **Volatile Matter:** 1 g sample (in covered crucible) → 950 °C for 7 min in inert atmosphere → reweigh; mass loss = VM.
4. **Ash:** Residue from VM → 750 °C with air access until constant mass.
5. **Fixed Carbon:** By difference.

**Typical Composition of Indian MSW:**

| Component | % by Weight (approx.) |
|---|---|
| Organic (food, yard) | 40 – 60 |
| Paper | 6 – 12 |
| Plastics | 8 – 15 |
| Glass | 4 – 8 |
| Metals | 2 – 5 |
| Textiles, Rubber, Leather | 4 – 8 |
| Inerts (Construction, etc.) | 8 – 15 |
| Moisture | 30 – 60 |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Landfill Calculations

**Given:** Daily MSW = 800 tonnes/day = 800,000 kg/day

**(i) Annual volume of compacted waste:**

**Mass per year:**
$$M_{annual} = 800 \times 365 = 292{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 292 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$$

**Annual compacted volume:**
$$V_{annual} = \dfrac{M_{annual}}{D_{compacted}} = \dfrac{292 \times 10^6}{750} = 389{,}333 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$

**(ii) Expected landfill life:**

**Total landfill volume:**
$$V_{total} = A \times d = (10 \times 10{,}000) \times 15 = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Landfill life:**
$$T_{life} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{annual}} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{389{,}333} = 3.85 \text{ years}$$

**(iii) Compaction ratio:**

$$CR = \dfrac{V_{loose}}{V_{compacted}} = \dfrac{D_{compacted}}{D_{loose}} = \dfrac{750}{250} = 3.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 3:1 compaction ratio is *typical* for sanitary landfill operations using tracked bulldozers in 0.5 m lifts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (Examiner's Note):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to "as-received" basis:** When the problem gives moisture content, students compute GCV/NCV on dry basis and stop. Always convert!
> 2. **Mixing up wet-basis and dry-basis MC:** Dry basis MC can exceed 100% — wet basis cannot.
> 3. **Using the wrong density for volume:** Many students use bulk density instead of compacted density (or vice versa) in landfill calculations.
> 4. **Confusing GCV and NCV:** If the question asks for *usable energy*, write NCV. If it asks for *heat of combustion*, write GCV.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Always write **kJ/kg** or **kcal/kg** — losing 1 mark per sub-question for missing units is common.
> 6. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (e.g., Dulong's equation assumes fuel composition; not valid for high-ash MSW without correction).
> 7. **In C/N ratio problems:** Always check whether the base material's ratio is given in mass or mass fraction before applying mass balance.
> 8. **Dulong's equation traps:** If O > H (as in many MSWs), the term $H - O/8$ may go negative. Always verify and comment.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Properties of Solid Wastes**
>
> ✅ **Three domains:** Physical, Chemical, Biological.
> 
> ✅ **Moisture Content:** Wet-basis ≤ 100%; Dry-basis can exceed 100%. Standard drying at **103–105 °C / 24 h**.
> 
> ✅ **Bulk vs Particle Density:** Bulk includes voids; Particle excludes voids. Used for vehicle and landfill design.
> 
> ✅ **Porosity:** $n = 1 - D_b/D_p$
> 
> ✅ **Proximate Analysis:** M + VM + FC + Ash = 100%. Volatiles are determined at 950 °C in **inert** atmosphere.
> 
> ✅ **Ultimate Analysis:** CHNOS + Ash; used with Dulong's equation.
> 
> ✅ **Dulong's Equation (in kJ/kg):** $GCV = 337C + 1418(H - O/8) + 93S$
> 
> ✅ **NCV Formula:** $NCV = GCV - 2260 \times (9H + M)/100$
> 
> ✅ **C/N Ratio:** Ideal compost = **25:1 to 35:1**. Low C/N = ammonia release; High C/N = slow composting.
> 
> ✅ **Incineration Threshold:** NCV > **5000 kJ/kg** (≈ 1200 kcal/kg) for self-sustaining combustion.
> 
> ✅ **Field Capacity:** Critical for landfill leachate design.
> 
> ✅ **Methane Yield:** Typical 0.4 – 0.5 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed in anaerobic digestion.
> 
> ✅ **Typical Indian MSW:** Moisture 40–60%, organic 40–60%, calorific value 800–1500 kJ/kg (low compared to developed countries).
> 
> ✅ **Scholl Canyon Model:** $Q = k \cdot L_0 \cdot M \cdot e^{-kt}$ — first-order landfill gas generation model (frequently tested).
> 
> ✅ **Permeability (Darcy's Law):** $Q = -kA \dfrac{dh}{dx}$ — governs leachate flow through waste mass.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Waste Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Waste Generation** refers to the stage in the *Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM)* hierarchy at which materials become valueless to the owner and are discarded, requiring systematic quantification, classification, and characterization to design appropriate collection, treatment, and disposal systems.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome **CO1**: *Identify the sources, types, and characteristics of solid wastes*), **waste generation** is the foundational input parameter upon which the entire downstream engineering design — from collection vehicle routing to landfill sizing — is based.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition — Solid Waste (Per UNEP & Indian CPCB):** Any discarded material arising from human or animal activities that is no longer valued by the original owner and is intended to be disposed of, reused, recycled, or treated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap & Sink" Model

Think of a city as a giant kitchen sink:
- **The Tap (Generation)** = People buying groceries, using products, throwing packaging. Every drop (waste item) that comes out of the tap enters the system.
- **The Drainage Pipes (Collection & Transport)** = Municipal waste pickers and trucks.
- **The Sink Basin (Storage/Processing)** = Transfer stations, MRFs, composting plants.
- **The U-Bend (Treatment)** = Incinerators, recycling centers.
- **The Outside Drain (Disposal)** = Landfills, open dumps.

> If you don't accurately measure **how much water the tap is pouring in (waste generation rate)**, you can never size the pipes, the sink, or the drain. **Waste generation is the "tap" of the entire SWM system.**

### 1.3 Classification of Solid Waste — KTU Module 1 Blueprint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid wastes are classified by SOURCE (origin) and by TYPE (physical/chemical nature). KTU examiners expect students to know both axes clearly.**

| Classification Axis | Categories | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **By Source** | Residential, Commercial, Institutional, Construction & Demolition (C&D), Municipal Services, Industrial, Agricultural, Biomedical | **High (8–10 marks)** |
| **By Type** | Organic/Biodegradable, Recyclable (paper, glass, metal, plastic), Inert (construction rubble), Hazardous, Biomedical, E-Waste | **High (8–10 marks)** |

### 1.4 Sources of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) — Detailed Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **MSW** = Municipal Solid Waste = waste collected by or for the municipal authority.

| # | Source | Typical Constituents | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Residential** | Food waste, paper, plastic, glass, textiles, yard waste | Household kitchen bins |
| 2 | **Commercial** | Paper, cardboard, packaging, food (from restaurants) | Offices, shops, hotels |
| 3 | **Institutional** | Paper, food, plastic, laboratory waste | Schools, hospitals, colleges |
| 4 | **Construction & Demolition** | Concrete, wood, metals, bricks, insulation | Building sites |
| 5 | **Municipal Services** | Street sweepings, parks, beach cleanup waste, STP sludge | Municipal operations |
| 6 | **Treatment Plant Residues** | Incinerator ash, compost rejects, sludge | Output of SWM plants |
| 7 | **Industrial** | Process waste, packaging, scrap | Factories (non-hazardous portion) |
| 8 | **Agricultural** | Crop residues, animal dung, plastics from farms | Rural areas |

### 1.5 Types of Solid Waste (By Nature)

1. **Biodegradable / Organic Waste** — Food, yard waste, paper (partly). Degrades naturally.
2. **Recyclable / Non-Biodegradable** — Plastics, glass, metals, rubber, textiles.
3. **Inert Waste** — Construction debris, sand, rubble. Does not react chemically.
4. **Hazardous Waste** — Toxic, flammable, reactive, corrosive. Regulated under **HWM Rules 2016 (amended 2018)**.
5. **Biomedical Waste** — Human/animal tissue, sharps, pharma waste. Regulated under **BMW Management Rules 2016**.
6. **Electronic Waste (E-Waste)** — TVs, computers, batteries. Regulated under **E-Waste Management Rules 2022**.
7. **Radioactive Waste** — From nuclear facilities. Highly regulated.
8. **Industrial Non-Hazardous** — Process tailings, fly ash (when not classified hazardous).
9. **Agricultural Waste** — Crop residue, animal waste.
10. **Municipal / Domestic Waste** — The combined stream from urban households.

### 1.6 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three fundamental units you MUST memorize for KTU Module 1:**
> - **kg/capita/day** — individual waste generation rate (most common)
> - **tonnes/day (TPD)** — community/city level rate
> - **% composition by weight** — waste characterization metric

### 1.7 Why "Waste Generation" is the Most Critical Module 1 Topic

Every numerical problem in SWM begins with one question: *"How much waste is being produced?"* Without a reliable answer, you cannot:
- Design container **storage volume** (Section 1.7 of MSW Rules 2000)
- Decide the **number of collection trips per day**
- Size the **transfer station** capacity
- Calculate the **landfill area** required
- Estimate **revenue from recycling** or **tipping fees**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Affecting Waste Generation Rate

> [!NOTE]
> **The per-capita waste generation rate is NOT a constant.** It varies dramatically with the parameters below. KTU frequently asks: *"Discuss the factors affecting waste generation."* (10–14 mark question).

#### 2.1.1 Socio-Economic Factors
- **Income level / Affluence**: Higher GDP per capita → higher waste generation. A New York resident generates ~ **2.0 kg/capita/day**, a rural Indian villager generates ~ **0.1–0.3 kg/capita/day**.
- **Education & Awareness**: Educated populations segregate and reduce waste.
- **Lifestyle & Consumption Patterns**: Urban, packaged-goods-heavy lifestyles generate more non-biodegradable waste.
- **Cultural & Religious Practices**: Festival seasons (Diwali in India, Christmas globally) cause 20–40% spikes.
- **Family Size**: Smaller families → more per-capita waste (smaller shared packaging economy).

#### 2.1.2 Climatic & Geographic Factors
- **Climate**: Hot climates → more food waste, more garden waste.
- **Season**: Summer → more garden waste, more beverages → more packaging.
- **Geography**: Coastal vs. inland, urban vs. rural.

#### 2.1.3 Technological & Economic Factors
- **Industrialization**: Increases industrial and packaging waste.
- **Availability of Alternatives**: Reusable bags, returnable bottles reduce waste.
- **Packaging Trends**: Growth of e-commerce → massive cardboard and plastic surge.
- **Tourism**: Tourist cities (Kerala: Munnar, Kochi) generate 30–50% more waste per resident equivalent.

#### 2.1.4 Policy & Regulatory Factors
- **MSW Rules 2000 (amended 2016, 2020)**: Mandate segregation at source.
- **Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2022)**: Ban on identified single-use plastics.
- **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) 2014+**: Behavioral change → improved collection but higher measured generation.

### 2.2 Quantitative Framework — The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU Module 1 numerical problems use one or more of the following formulas. Memorize them in the exact form below.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Per Capita Waste Generation** | $q = \dfrac{W_{total}}{P \times t}$ | $q$ = per capita rate (kg/cap/day), $W_{total}$ = total waste (kg), $P$ = population, $t$ = time (days) | Foundation formula for all MSW design |
| 2 | **Total Waste Generated (Projected)** | $W_{future} = q \times P_{future} \times t$ | $W$ in kg or tonnes | Future MSW estimation |
| 3 | **Population Forecast (Arithmetic Growth)** | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | $P_n$ = population after $n$ years, $P_0$ = base population, $\bar{x}$ = arithmetic mean of decadal growth (persons/decade) | Used for towns of < 1 lakh population |
| 4 | **Population Forecast (Geometric Growth)** | $P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r/100)^n$ | $r$ = geometric mean growth rate (% per decade), $n$ = number of decades | Used for cities of 1–50 lakh population |
| 5 | **Population Forecast (Exponential Growth)** | $P_n = P_0 \cdot e^{k n}$ | $k$ = exponential growth constant = $\ln(1+r/100)$ | Used for metro cities > 50 lakh, e.g., Mumbai, Delhi |
| 6 | **Volume-to-Weight Conversion** | $W = V \times d$ | $V$ = volume (m³), $d$ = bulk density (kg/m³), $W$ = weight (kg) | Needed for container design |
| 7 | **Mass Density of MSW (Typical)** | $d_{MSW} = 300$ to $\mathbf{500}$ kg/m³ (loose); $600$ to $\mathbf{900}$ kg/m³ (compacted) | — | For Indian MSW |
| 8 | **Waste Composition (% by weight)** | $\%_{comp} = \dfrac{W_{comp}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | $W_{comp}$ = weight of component (kg) | Waste characterization |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL (KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern):** When using the **Geometric Growth** formula, the growth rate $r$ must be expressed **per decade**, not per year, when dealing with census data (Indian census is decadal). Misinterpreting this costs **2–3 marks** per problem.

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Container Sizing (Street Bins, Community Bins)**: Volume = $W / d$ (must add 25% safety factor).
- **Collection Vehicle Capacity**: Truck trips = $\lceil W_{daily} / C_{truck} \rceil$, where $C_{truck}$ is truck payload.
- **Landfill Sizing**: Area = $W_{lifetime} / (d_{compacted} \times H)$, where $H$ is landfill height.
- **Composting Plant Capacity**: Biodegradable tonnage = $W_{daily} \times \%_{biodegradable}$.
- **Incinerator Capacity**: Calorific content check uses non-biodegradable, dry fraction.

### 2.4 Indian Context — Standard Per Capita Values (CPCB Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **CPCB Recommended Per Capita Waste Generation (Indian Cities):**
> - **Class I cities (Metro, > 1 lakh):** 0.50 to 0.60 kg/cap/day
> - **Class II cities (50,000 to 1 lakh):** 0.40 to 0.50 kg/cap/day
> - **Towns (< 50,000):** 0.20 to 0.40 kg/cap/day
> - **Rural areas:** 0.10 to 0.30 kg/cap/day
> - **Kerala average (2020 estimate):** ~ **0.45 kg/cap/day** (slightly above national average due to non-biodegradable packaging from food & beverages)

### 2.5 Concept of Waste Generation Rate (WGR) vs. Per Capita

There are **three levels** of waste generation rate:

1. **Individual (Per Capita) Rate ($q$)** — kg/cap/day. Unit: $\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{capita} \cdot \text{day}}$
2. **Daily Generation Rate** — kg/day or tonnes/day (TPD) for a community.
3. **Annual Generation Rate** — tonnes/year, used for landfill design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Mastery:** $1 \text{ TPD} = 1000 \text{ kg/day} = 1000 \times 365 \text{ kg/year} = 365 \text{ tonnes/year}$

### 2.6 Waste Generation Hierarchy (Engineering Priority)

When designing an SWM system, the **waste generation data** must be analyzed at the following granularity:

| Level | Granularity | Data Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone Level** | District / Ward | Municipal records, GIS |
| **Building Level** | Residential / Commercial / Institutional | On-site audits |
| **Door-to-Door Level** | Household bins | Direct weighbridge surveys |
| **Generation Point** | Single bin, single event | High-resolution studies |

This is the **"waste generation audit"** students will be expected to plan in KTU design problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Per Capita Waste Generation Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 3-mark Part A question:** *"Define per capita waste generation and write its formula."*

**Step 1 — Define the underlying principle.**
The per capita waste generation rate is the **time-averaged weight of solid waste generated per person per unit time** in a defined community. It assumes uniform behaviour across the population, which is a first-order engineering approximation.

**Step 2 — State the basic relationship.**

$$
W_{total} = q \times P \times t
$$

where:
- $W_{total}$ = total waste generated (kg)
- $q$ = per capita generation rate (kg/cap/day)
- $P$ = population served (capita)
- $t$ = time period (days)

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $q$.**

$$
q = \frac{W_{total}}{P \times t}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check on units.**

$$
q = \frac{[\text{kg}]}{[\text{capita}] \times [\text{day}]} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{cap} \cdot \text{day}} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering extension: Future projection.**

If the population grows to $P_n$ after $n$ years and lifestyle shifts the per capita rate to $q_n$:

$$
W_{n, daily} = q_n \times P_n
$$

$$
W_{n, annual} = q_n \times P_n \times 365
$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form for landfill design (lifetime $L$ years).**

$$
W_{lifetime} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} q_i \cdot P_i \cdot 365
$$

If $q$ and $P$ are assumed linear:

$$
W_{lifetime} \approx 365 \cdot \frac{(q_1 P_1 + q_L P_L)}{2} \cdot L
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Geometric Mean Growth Rate ($r_{geom}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 3-mark question:** *"The population of a town in three successive decades was 50,000, 65,000, and 84,500. Find the geometric mean growth rate."*

**Step 1 — Definition of geometric mean growth rate.**

Given population $P_0$ in decade 0 and $P_n$ in decade $n$:

$$
P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r/100)^n
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $r$.**

$$
(1 + r/100)^n = \frac{P_n}{P_0}
$$

$$
1 + r/100 = \left(\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n}
$$

$$
r = 100 \cdot \left[ \left(\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula to the example.**

Given: $P_0 = 50{,}000$, $P_2 = 84{,}500$, $n = 2$ decades.

$$
r = 100 \cdot \left[ \left(\frac{84{,}500}{50{,}000}\right)^{1/2} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
r = 100 \cdot \left[ (1.69)^{0.5} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
r = 100 \cdot \left[ 1.30 - 1 \right] = 30\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verify.** $50{,}000 \times (1.30)^2 = 50{,}000 \times 1.69 = 84{,}500$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Waste Composition Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 14-mark question pattern:** *"From the data below, calculate the percentage composition of MSW and comment on its suitability for composting/incineration."*

**Step 1 — Set up the data table.**

| Component | Weight (kg) |
|---|---|
| Food waste | 120 |
| Paper & Cardboard | 80 |
| Plastics | 60 |
| Glass | 20 |
| Metals | 15 |
| Textiles | 10 |
| Yard waste | 35 |
| Inert (dust, etc.) | 60 |
| **Total** | **400** |

**Step 2 — Apply the percentage formula for each component.**

$$
\%_{component} = \frac{W_{component}}{W_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each value.**

$$
\%_{food} = \frac{120}{400} \times 100 = 30.0\%
$$

$$
\%_{paper} = \frac{80}{400} \times 100 = 20.0\%
$$

$$
\%_{plastic} = \frac{60}{400} \times 100 = 15.0\%
$$

$$
\%_{glass} = \frac{20}{400} \times 100 = 5.0\%
$$

$$
\%_{metal} = \frac{15}{400} \times 100 = 3.75\%
$$

$$
\%_{textile} = \frac{10}{400} \times 100 = 2.5\%
$$

$$
\%_{yard} = \frac{35}{400} \times 100 = 8.75\%
$$

$$
\%_{inert} = \frac{60}{400} \times 100 = 15.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the sum.**

$$
30.0 + 20.0 + 15.0 + 5.0 + 3.75 + 2.5 + 8.75 + 15.0 = 100.0\% \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

- **Biodegradable fraction** (Food + Paper + Yard + Textiles) = $30 + 20 + 8.75 + 2.5 = 61.25\%$
- **Recyclable fraction** (Paper + Plastic + Glass + Metal) = $20 + 15 + 5 + 3.75 = 43.75\%$
- **Inert fraction** = $15\%$

> **Verdict:** High biodegradable content ($> 50\%$) → **Composting is viable** as a primary treatment.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: From Generation Rate to Landfill Area

**Step 1 — Establish the chain of dependencies.**

$$
A_{landfill} = \frac{W_{lifetime}}{d_{compacted} \times H_{landfill} \times 0.75}
$$

The factor **0.75** accounts for **soil cover** (typically 1:4 cover-to-waste ratio, plus base liner and operational areas).

**Step 2 — Expand $W_{lifetime}$ assuming constant $q$ and $P$.**

$$
A_{landfill} = \frac{q \cdot P \cdot 365 \cdot L}{d_{compacted} \cdot H \cdot 0.75}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example.**

A town with:
- $P = 200{,}000$
- $q = 0.5$ kg/cap/day
- $L = 20$ years design life
- $d_{compacted} = 700$ kg/m³
- $H = 15$ m landfill height

$$
A_{landfill} = \frac{0.5 \times 200{,}000 \times 365 \times 20}{700 \times 15 \times 0.75}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator calculation.**

$$
\text{Num} = 0.5 \times 200{,}000 \times 365 \times 20 = 7.3 \times 10^{8} \text{ kg} = 730{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator calculation.**

$$
\text{Den} = 700 \times 15 \times 0.75 = 7{,}875 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Final area.**

$$
A_{landfill} = \frac{7.3 \times 10^{8}}{7{,}875} = 92{,}698 \text{ m}^2 \approx 9.27 \text{ hectares}
$$

**Step 7 — Practical addition.** Add **20%** for buffer zones, drainage, leachate ponds, and access roads:

$$
A_{final} = 92{,}698 \times 1.20 = 111{,}238 \text{ m}^2 \approx 11.1 \text{ hectares}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Waste Generation Data Interpretation

| Observation | Likely Implication | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| $q > 1.0$ kg/cap/day | Highly affluent area / tourist hotspot / commercial dominant | Increase collection frequency, add segregation infrastructure |
| $q < 0.2$ kg/cap/day | Rural area / low-income / under-reported | Promote source segregation, awareness, baseline surveys |
| Biodegradable $> 60\%$ | Good for composting | Invest in composting / biogas plant |
| Plastic $> 15\%$ | Non-biodegradable dominant | Material Recovery Facility (MRF) prioritisation |
| Inert $> 25\%$ | High C&D waste | Separate C&D stream |
| Hazardous detected | Improper segregation | Hazardous waste treatment linkage |

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem — Complete Model Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive exam-style problem (will appear in Part B).**

**Problem:** *The population of a town is 1,20,000. The quantity of solid waste generated is 60 tonnes/day. The composition of waste is: biodegradable 50%, recyclable 25%, inert 20%, and hazardous 5% (by weight).*
>
> *Calculate:*
> *(a) Per capita waste generation rate.*
> *(b) The annual biodegradable waste generated in tonnes.*
> *(c) Volume of biodegradable waste generated per year, if its bulk density is 400 kg/m³.*
> *(d) Number of composting windrows of size 3 m × 1.5 m × 30 m (length) required, assuming one windrow holds 14 days' waste.*

**Step (a):** Per capita rate.

$$
q = \frac{W}{P} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{1{,}20{,}000} = 0.5 \text{ kg/cap/day}
$$

**Step (b):** Annual biodegradable waste.

$$
W_{bio, annual} = 60 \text{ TPD} \times 0.50 \times 365 = 10{,}950 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step (c):** Volume of biodegradable waste per year.

$$
V_{bio} = \frac{W_{bio}}{d} = \frac{10{,}950 \times 1000 \text{ kg}}{400 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 27{,}375 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**Step (d):** Number of windrows.

- Daily biodegradable waste volume: $V_{day} = \frac{60 \times 0.50 \times 1000}{400} = 75 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$
- 14-day accumulation volume: $V_{14d} = 75 \times 14 = 1050 \text{ m}^3$
- Single windrow volume: $V_{windrow} = 3 \times 1.5 \times 30 = 135 \text{ m}^3$

$$
N_{windrows} = \frac{1050}{135} = 7.78 \approx 8 \text{ windrows}
$$

> **Final Answer (KTU-style boxed):** $q = 0.5$ kg/cap/day; $W_{bio,annual} = 10{,}950$ T; $V_{bio,annual} = 27{,}375$ m³; $N_{windrows} = 8$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**Define per capita solid waste generation. List any four factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**
Per capita solid waste generation is defined as the average weight of solid waste generated per person per day in a given area. Mathematically:

$$
q = \frac{W_{total}}{P \times t}
$$

where $q$ is in kg/cap/day, $W_{total}$ is total waste in kg, $P$ is population, and $t$ is the time period in days.

**Four factors affecting it:**
1. Income level / standard of living of the population
2. Climatic and seasonal variations
3. Level of industrialization and packaging intensity
4. Government policies and regulations (e.g., Plastic Waste Management Rules)

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`
**Differentiate between Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) and Biomedical Waste.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MSW | Biomedical Waste |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Households, commercial, institutions | Hospitals, clinics, labs |
| Composition | Mostly biodegradable + recyclables | Pathological, pharmaceutical, sharps |
| Regulated under | MSW Rules 2000 (amended) | BMW Management Rules 2016 |
| Risk | Low to moderate | High (infectious, toxic) |
| Treatment | Composting, landfill | Autoclave, incineration |
| Colour-coded bins (typical) | Green (wet), Blue (dry) | Yellow, Red, White, Blue |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Module 1]`

**(a)** Classify solid waste based on (i) source and (ii) type, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city has a population of 8,00,000 and generates 480 tonnes/day of MSW. The composition is: biodegradable 55%, paper 12%, plastic 14%, glass 4%, metals 3%, inert 10%, and hazardous 2%. Determine:
1. The per capita waste generation rate.
2. The total annual waste generated in tonnes.
3. The annual mass and volume of biodegradable waste if its bulk density is 350 kg/m³.
4. Comment on the suitability of composting as a treatment method. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Classification:**

*(i) By Source:* Residential, Commercial, Institutional, Construction & Demolition, Municipal Services, Industrial, Agricultural, Biomedical, E-Waste.

*(ii) By Type:* Biodegradable (food, yard waste), Recyclable (paper, plastic, glass, metal), Inert (construction rubble, sand), Hazardous (batteries, paints), Biomedical, E-Waste, Radioactive.

**Part (b) — Calculations:**

**Step 1 — Per capita rate.**

$$
q = \frac{480 \times 1000 \text{ kg/day}}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.6 \text{ kg/cap/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual waste.**

$$
W_{annual} = 480 \times 365 = 1{,}75{,}200 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Biodegradable fraction.**

$$
W_{bio, annual} = 1{,}75{,}200 \times 0.55 = 96{,}360 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

$$
= 96{,}360 \times 1000 = 9.636 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Volume of biodegradable waste.**

$$
V_{bio, annual} = \frac{9.636 \times 10^{7}}{350} = 2.753 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^3/\text{year} \approx 2{,}75{,}314 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**Step 5 — Suitability of composting:**

With biodegradable content at **55% by weight**, the city is well-suited for composting. Since biodegradable $> 50\%$, composting (aerobic or anaerobic digestion) is the preferred primary treatment. The inorganic fractions (plastic, glass, metal) can be sent to a Material Recovery Facility (MRF).

---

#### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the various factors affecting per capita solid waste generation in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The population of a town in three successive decades was 40,000, 52,000, and 67,600. Calculate:
1. The arithmetic mean growth rate per decade.
2. The geometric mean growth rate per decade.
3. The expected population after 30 years using the geometric method.
4. The expected daily MSW generation in tonnes if the per capita rate is 0.5 kg/cap/day. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Factors affecting per capita waste generation:**

1. **Socio-Economic Factors:** Income, education, family size, lifestyle, and cultural practices directly influence consumption and disposal patterns.
2. **Geographical/Climatic Factors:** Climate, season, rural-urban status, and tourism intensity.
3. **Technological Factors:** Industrialization, packaging trends (especially e-commerce), and availability of alternatives.
4. **Policy/Regulatory Factors:** MSW Rules 2000, Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016, and Swachh Bharat Mission influence waste composition and reported quantities.

**Part (b) — Calculations:**

**Step 1 — Arithmetic mean growth rate.**

Decadal increases: $52{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 12{,}000$ and $67{,}600 - 52{,}000 = 15{,}600$.

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{12{,}000 + 15{,}600}{2} = 13{,}800 \text{ persons/decade}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric mean growth rate.**

Using $P_n = P_0 (1 + r/100)^n$ with $P_0 = 40{,}000$, $P_2 = 67{,}600$, $n = 2$:

$$
(1 + r/100)^2 = \frac{67{,}600}{40{,}000} = 1.69
$$

$$
1 + r/100 = \sqrt{1.69} = 1.30
$$

$$
r = 30\% \text{ per decade}
$$

**Step 3 — Population after 30 years (3 decades from base).**

$$
P_{30} = 40{,}000 \times (1.30)^3 = 40{,}000 \times 2.197 = 87{,}880 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily MSW generation.**

$$
W_{daily} = q \times P = 0.5 \times 87{,}880 = 43{,}940 \text{ kg/day} \approx 43.94 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg (×1000) or years to days (×365). Always show units at every step.
> 2. **Decade vs Year Confusion (–2 to 3 marks):** When using the geometric mean growth formula, the rate is per decade, not per year. The exponential form $e^{kn}$ uses the **per-year** rate.
> 3. **Composition Percentage Error (–1 to 2 marks):** A common mistake is forgetting to multiply by the total weight when computing component-wise mass. Always write: $W_{comp} = W_{total} \times (\%_{comp}/100)$.
> 4. **Missing Interpretation (–2 marks):** KTU examiners award full marks ONLY if you **interpret** the result. Example: After computing biodegradable % as 55%, you MUST state "**composting is viable**" or "**high biodegradability**" — not just write the number.
> 5. **Segregation of Hazardous (–1 mark):** A common omission is failing to highlight that **hazardous waste should NOT enter the composting/incineration MSW stream** and must be sent to a TSDF.
> 6. **No Mention of Indian Standards (–1 mark):** Referencing CPCB/MoEFCC rules (e.g., MSW Rules 2000, BMW Rules 2016) adds **at least 1 mark of impression bonus**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — WASTE GENERATION (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Waste generation = materials discarded by owner; first stage of SWM hierarchy.
- ✅ **Two classification axes:** **By Source** (Residential, Commercial, Institutional, Industrial, C&D, Municipal, Agricultural, Biomedical, E-Waste) AND **By Type** (Biodegradable, Recyclable, Inert, Hazardous, Biomedical, E-Waste, Radioactive).
- ✅ **Per capita formula:** $q = W_{total} / (P \times t)$ in **kg/cap/day**.
- ✅ **Population forecasting methods:**
  - **Arithmetic:** For towns < 1 lakh. $P_n = P_0 + n \bar{x}$
  - **Geometric:** For 1–50 lakh. $P_n = P_0 (1 + r/100)^n$ — $r$ is **per decade**
  - **Exponential:** For > 50 lakh. $P_n = P_0 e^{kn}$
- ✅ **CPCB per capita values (India):** 0.10–0.60 kg/cap/day depending on class.
- ✅ **Key conversions:** 1 TPD = 1000 kg/day = 365 tonnes/year.
- ✅ **MSW bulk density (India):** Loose = 300–500 kg/m³; Compacted = 600–900 kg/m³.
- ✅ **Four factor categories:** Socio-Economic, Climatic, Technological, Policy/Regulatory.
- ✅ **Engineering use chain:** Generation → $q$ → Daily W → Annual W → Landfill Area / Composting Capacity / MRF Capacity.
- ✅ **Landfill area formula:** $A = W_{lifetime} / (d_{compacted} \times H \times 0.75)$ + 20% for buffer.
- ✅ **Composition interpretation rule:** If biodegradable $> 50\%$ → composting viable. If plastic $> 15\%$ → MRF priority. If hazardous detected → TSDF linkage mandatory.
- ✅ **Indian rules to remember:** MSW Rules 2000 (amended 2016, 2020), BMW Rules 2016, PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022), E-Waste Rules 2022, HWM Rules 2016.
- ✅ **Always interpret results** in numerical problems — KTU examiners expect a concluding engineering statement.

---

**End of Module 1 — Waste Generation Notes (PECHT861, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
