# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define incineration. State the 3-T rule and its significance in incinerator design.**

**Model Answer:**

Incineration is the controlled thermal oxidation of combustible solid waste at high temperature (850–1200 °C) in the presence of excess air to reduce volume, mass, and recover energy.

The 3-T rule specifies the three design parameters governing combustion quality:

1. **Temperature:** Combustion chamber temperature should be maintained at **≥ 850 °C** (preferably 1000–1200 °C) to ensure destruction of pathogens and prevention of dioxin formation.
2. **Time:** Flue gas residence time at this temperature should be **≥ 2 seconds** to ensure complete oxidation of CO and unburnt hydrocarbons.
3. **Turbulence:** Adequate mixing between fuel and air must be ensured through proper grate design, secondary air injection, and chamber geometry.

Significance: Maintaining the 3-T rule ensures > 99 % combustion efficiency, minimizes CO and dioxin emissions, and satisfies CPCB statutory norms. *(3 marks: 1 def + 2 for 3-T explanation)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Mass-Burn and RDF-fired incinerators.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mass-Burn | RDF-Fired |
|---|---|---|
| Feed form | As-received MSW (no preprocessing) | Pre-shredded, dried, densified pellets/fluff |
| Capacity | Large (> 200 TPD) | Medium to large (50–1000 TPD) |
| Moisture tolerance | Handles 40–55 % moisture | Requires waste dried to < 20 % moisture |
| Capital cost | High (grate + refractory) | Lower (boiler-grade fuel) |
| Auxiliary fuel | Often required in cold start | Seldom required |
| Heat value input | Variable (5–12 MJ/kg) | Stable (15–20 MJ/kg) |
| Application | Urban municipal plants | Cement kilns, captive power plants |

*(3 marks: comparison + key distinguishing feature)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A municipal solid waste sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass (dry basis): C = 42 %, H = 6 %, O = 32 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 1 %, Ash = 7.5 %, Moisture = 30 % (wet basis). Calculate:
- (i) Stoichiometric oxygen required per kg of dry waste.
- (ii) Theoretical air required per kg of dry waste.
- (iii) Actual air required when excess air is 120 %.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the LHV of the above waste using Dulong's formula. State whether the waste is self-sustaining in an incinerator.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a)

**(i) Stoichiometric oxygen:**

Using $O_2^{stoich} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1.0\,S - 1.0\,O$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{stoich} &= 2.667 \times 0.42 + 8 \times 0.06 + 1.0 \times 0.01 - 1.0 \times 0.32 \\[4pt]
&= 1.120 + 0.480 + 0.010 - 0.320 \\[4pt]
&= 1.290\ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical air:**

$$
A_{th} = \frac{1.290}{0.232} = 5.560\ \text{kg air / kg dry waste}
$$

**(iii) Actual air at 120 % excess ($e = 1.20$):**

$$
A_{actual} = 5.560 \times (1 + 1.20) = 5.560 \times 2.20 = 12.232\ \text{kg air / kg dry waste}
$$

#### Part (b)

**LHV (Dulong, dry basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{dry} &= 33{,}850 \times 0.42 + 144{,}200 \times \left(0.06 - \frac{0.32}{8}\right) + 9410 \times 0.01 \\[4pt]
&= 14{,}217 + 144{,}200 \times (0.06 - 0.04) + 94.1 \\[4pt]
&= 14{,}217 + 144{,}200 \times 0.02 + 94.1 \\[4pt]
&= 14{,}217 + 2{,}884 + 94.1 \\[4pt]
&= 17{,}195.1\ \text{kJ/kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**LHV (wet basis, accounting for 30 % moisture, 0.30 kg water per kg wet waste, dry fraction = 0.70):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{wet} &= 17{,}195 \times 0.70 - 2450 \times 0.30 \\[4pt]
&= 12{,}036.5 - 735 \\[4pt]
&= 11{,}301.5\ \text{kJ/kg wet waste} \approx 11.30\ \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $LHV_{wet} = 11.30$ MJ/kg **> 5 MJ/kg**, the waste is **self-sustaining** and does not require auxiliary fuel support. *(7 marks: 4 for LHV dry + 2 for wet + 1 for conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a **mass-burn incinerator** with energy recovery. List its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Air Pollution Control (APC)** train in a modern WtE plant. Mention the typical Indian CPCB emission norms for PM, SO₂, NOx, HCl, and dioxins.

**Model Solution Outline:**

#### Part (a)

- **Sketch:** Feed hopper → inclined grate furnace (drying, pyrolysis, combustion, burnout zones) → secondary chamber → waste-heat boiler → ESP → scrubber → stack; bottom-ash conveyor.
- **Working:** MSW charged onto grate; primary air enters from below; secondary air injected above the bed; flue gas at 1000 °C passes to boiler generating 40 bar, 400 °C steam; steam drives turbine-generator; bottom ash quenched and metal-recovered.
- **Advantages:** No preprocessing; high volume reduction (90 %); generates electricity; suitable for large urban plants.
- **Limitations:** High capital cost; sensitive to waste composition; produces toxic fly ash requiring secure landfill.

#### Part (b)

- **APC Train:** SNCR (Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction for NOx) → dry lime injection (HCl/SO₂) → activated carbon (Hg, dioxins) → baghouse filter (PM) → wet scrubber polish → continuous emissions monitoring system (CEMS).
- **Typical CPCB Norms (WtE plants):**
  - PM < **30 mg/Nm³**
  - SO₂ < **200 mg/Nm³**
  - NOx < **400 mg/Nm³**
  - HCl < **50 mg/Nm³**
  - Dioxins/Furans < **0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³**
  - CO < **50 mg/Nm³** (24-h average)

*(7 marks: 3 sketch + 2 working + 2 APC explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in Dulong's formula** — students often forget the negative term $-O/8$ for H. The correct form is $144{,}200 \times (H - O/8)$, not $144{,}200 \times H + 144{,}200 \times O/8$. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
> 2. **Using 0.21 instead of 0.232 for air composition** — air is 23.2 % O₂ by *mass* and 21 % by *volume*. Wrong choice → wrong answer. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 3. **Excess air formula** — excess air is the air *in excess of stoichiometric*, so actual air = theoretical × (1 + e/100). If a student writes (1 + e) but e is given as percent, the value is 100× wrong. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 4. **Skipping the unit conversion from dry to wet LHV** — KTU board expects the *final answer in MJ/kg of wet waste*, not dry. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
> 5. **No sketch in mass-burn question** — board examiners in KTU deduct up to **2 marks** if the diagram is missing or unlabeled.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Incineration** = controlled thermal oxidation; 80–90 % volume reduction.
- **3-T Rule:** Temperature (≥ 850 °C), Time (≥ 2 s gas residence), Turbulence (good mixing).
- **Six main types:** Mass-burn, Modular, RDF-fired, FBC, Rotary Kiln, Hearth.
- **Stoichiometric O₂:** $O_2^{stoich} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O$ (kg O₂ / kg dry waste).
- **Theoretical air:** $A_{th} = O_2^{stoich}\, /\, 0.232$ (use **0.232 for mass, 0.21 for volume**).
- **Actual air with excess %:** $A_{actual} = A_{th} \times (1 + e/100)$.
- **Dulong's LHV:** $33{,}850\,C + 144{,}200\,(H - O/8) + 9410\,S$ in **kJ/kg dry**.
- **Self-sustaining waste:** $LHV_{wet} > 5$ MJ/kg → no auxiliary fuel needed.
- **Combustion efficiency indicator:** $\eta = [\text{CO}_2] / ([\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]) \times 100\ \%$.
- **CPCB emission limits (WtE):** PM < 30, SO₂ < 200, NOx < 400, HCl < 50, CO < 50 mg/Nm³; Dioxins < 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³.
- **Residues:** bottom ash (~25 % by mass, recyclable in road base), fly ash (~3–5 %, hazardous, secure landfill).
- **Energy recovery:** 1 ton MSW → ~ 0.5–1.0 MWh electricity in modern WtE plants.
- **Auxiliary fuel** is required during cold start or for low-LHV waste (< 5 MJ/kg).
- **Excess air** of 80–150 % is the KTU standard range for MSW incinerators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grate (Combustion Grate / Incinerator Grate):** A refractory-lined, air-permeable hearth or platform forming the floor of a combustion chamber of a *mass-burn incinerator*, on which Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) is progressively dried, ignited, combusted, and reduced to ash, while primary combustion air is supplied from beneath through air nozzles. The grate simultaneously supports the waste bed, transports it through the furnace, and agitates it for better burnout.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **PECHT861 – Solid Waste Management, Module 3 (Thermal Treatment & Incineration)**, a grate is the **heart of a moving-bed mass-burn system** for MSW, Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF), and other heterogeneous solid wastes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Grate like a moving BBQ rack on a conveyor belt, but in reverse orientation.**
>
> Imagine a *barbecue grill* in your backyard: charcoal sits on metal bars, hot air rises from below, ash falls through the gaps, and you use tongs to flip the food. A combustion grate is the **industrial-scale, mechanized version**:
> - The "metal bars" are water-cooled cast-iron or alloy tines (called **grate bars** or **fire bars**).
> - The "tongs" are replaced by a mechanized reciprocating or rotating motion that automatically turns and advances the waste bed.
> - The "hot air from below" is **primary combustion air**, precisely metered to dry, ignite, and burn the waste.
> - The "conveyor" moves the burning waste from the charging end (feed chute) to the ash discharge end.

Just like a barbecue must keep turning food for *even cooking*, a grate must continually agitate MSW because waste is a wildly heterogeneous fuel — wet paper, plastic, garden clippings, food waste, demolition debris — all mixed in random proportions.

## 1.3 Role of the Grate in the Incineration Process

A grate performs **five simultaneous functions** during combustion:

| S.No. | Function | Description |
|:-----:|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Transport** | Mechanically conveys waste from feed hopper to ash discharger |
| 2 | **Agitation** | Turns and mixes the bed for uniform burnout and exposure to air |
| 3 | **Air Distribution** | Delivers primary combustion air uniformly through the waste bed |
| 4 | **Combustion Zoning** | Creates distinct zones: drying → ignition → combustion → burnout |
| 5 | **Ash Removal** | Discharges bottom ash, clinker, and inert residues at the end |

## 1.4 Position in the Mass-Burn Incineration Train

The grate is sandwiched between upstream and downstream unit operations:

> **[ Feed Hopper / Ram Feeder ]** → **GRATE (Combustion Chamber)** → **[ Secondary Combustion Chamber / Post-Combustion Zone ]** → **[ Heat Recovery Boiler ]** → **[ Air Pollution Control ]**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Combustion Grates

KTU examiners expect a *clear taxonomy*. Grates are classified along three axes:

### (A) Based on Motion / Movement

| Type | Motion | Application |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **Stationary (Fixed) Grate** | No motion; manual poking | Small-scale, batch operations (< 1 TPD) |
| **Moving/Traveling Grate** | Continuous belt of cast-iron links | Continuous medium-scale plants |
| **Reciprocating Grate** | Alternate back-and-forth stroke | Most common for MSW (Martin, Von Roll) |
| **Rocking Grate** | Slow rotational rocking around axis | Older designs, drying of sludge |
| **Rotary Kiln** | Rotating cylindrical drum | Hazardous waste, sludges, RDF |
| **Fluidized Bed** | Bed of sand kept suspended in air | Uniform, shredded waste, RDF |

### (B) Based on Cooling Mechanism

| Type | Cooling Medium | Advantages |
|------|----------------|------------|
| **Air-Cooled Grate** | Forced ambient air | Simpler; pre-heats combustion air |
| **Water-Cooled Grate** | Circulating water in cast tines | Withstands higher temperatures (> 1100 °C); protects metal from slagging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Modern MSW incinerators > 5 TPD almost universally use **water-cooled reciprocating grates** because they tolerate the highly variable, often sticky and clinker-forming nature of Indian and global MSW.

### (C) Based on Inclination

| Type | Angle to Horizontal | Application |
|------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Horizontal / Stepped** | 0° – 15° | Uniform, shredded waste |
| **Inclined** | 15° – 30° | Most MSW plants |
| **Steeply Inclined** | > 30° | High-moisture waste |

## 2.2 Combustion Zones on the Grate

A modern grate physically partitions the fire bed into **four sequential zones**, typically demarcated by under-grate air compartments (zones I–IV):

| Zone | Location on Grate | Process | Temperature (°C) | Air Fraction |
|:----:|:-----------------:|---------|:-----------------:|:------------:|
| **I – Drying** | Front 25–30 % length | Moisture evaporation, water release | 200 – 600 | 25 – 30 % |
| **II – Ignition / Pyrolysis** | 25–50 % | Volatiles released, char begins forming | 500 – 900 | 20 – 25 % |
| **III – Combustion / Burnout** | 50–80 % | Char oxidation, peak flame | 900 – 1200 | 30 – 40 % |
| **IV – Ash Cooling** | Last 20 % | Residual burnout, ash quenching | 600 – 800 | 10 – 15 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The under-grate air is **not equally distributed** across the grate length — it is **zone-controlled** by dampers and fans to match the stoichiometric demand of each zone. Mis-zoning is the most common cause of incomplete burnout and high CO emissions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are **frequently tested** for numerical problems (3–7 mark sub-parts).

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|:-:|---------|-------------|:-------------:|
| 1 | $q_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{A_{grate}}$ | **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR)** — heat liberated per unit grate area | $\text{MJ/m}^{2}\cdot\text{h}$ or $\text{kW/m}^{2}$ |
| 2 | $t_{res} = \dfrac{L_{grate}}{v_{grate}}$ | **Residence time** of waste on grate | minutes or hours |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_{air,\,stoich} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste}}{0.232} \times \left( C + 0.375 S - 0.267 O \right)$ | Stoichiometric air (by mass) for combustible analysis | Nm³/h |
| 4 | $\dot{V}_{air,\,actual} = n \times \dot{V}_{air,\,stoich}$ | Actual air with excess ratio $n \approx 1.6 - 2.2$ | Nm³/h |
| 5 | $A_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{q_{grate,\,design}}$ | Grate area required for a given waste throughput | m² |
| 6 | $\eta_{burnout} = 1 - \dfrac{L_{LOI}}{100}$ | Combustion efficiency via Loss-On-Ignition of ash | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{primary} = (0.4 \text{ to } 0.7) \times \dot{V}_{air,\,actual}$ | Primary (under-grate) air share | Nm³/h |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{secondary} = \dot{V}_{air,\,actual} - \dot{Q}_{primary}$ | Secondary (over-fire) air injected in the post-combustion zone | Nm³/h |
| 9 | $\tau_{stroke} = \dfrac{L_{stroke}}{v_{avg,\,forward}}$ | Time between forward strokes of a reciprocating grate | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU design values to memorize:**
> - GHRR for MSW: **250 – 500 kW/m²** (≈ 0.9 – 1.8 GJ/m²·h)
> - Excess air ratio: **1.6 – 2.2**
> - Primary air share: **40 – 70 %** of stoichiometric
> - Grate residence time: **30 – 90 minutes** (depends on waste calorific value and grate load)
> - Grate load (specific waste load): **150 – 400 kg/m²·h**
> - Under-grate air velocity through tine gaps: **5 – 15 m/s**
> - Maximum allowable under-grate velocity to prevent fly-ash entrainment: **< 1.5 m/s**

## 2.4 Design Variables of a Reciprocating Grate

A *reciprocating grate* consists of several rows of cast-iron grate bars arranged laterally. Each row is fixed to a movable carrier that undergoes a slow forward stroke (advancing the waste) followed by a rapid backward stroke (which leaves the waste in place, creating agitation). The grate geometry is defined by:

- **Bar length** $L_b$ (1.0 – 1.5 m)
- **Bar width** $w_b$ (50 – 100 mm)
- **Bar spacing / gap** $g_b$ (8 – 15 mm) — controls air distribution and ash fall-through
- **Effective air opening ratio** $\alpha = \dfrac{g_b}{g_b + w_b} \approx 0.10 - 0.20$
- **Stroke length** $s$ (100 – 300 mm)
- **Stroke frequency** $f$ (2 – 8 strokes/min)
- **Forward velocity** $v_f = s \times f$ (typical: 1 – 5 m/h)

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Grate Area from Heat Release Rate

The **design of grate area** is the most frequently asked numerical problem in KTU examinations. The complete derivation is shown below.

**Given Data:**
- Mass feed rate of waste: $\dot{m}_{waste}$ in $\text{kg/h}$
- Net Calorific Value (NCV) of waste: $NCV$ in $\text{kJ/kg}$
- Design Grate Heat Release Rate: $q_{grate,\,design}$ in $\text{kW/m}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Convert mass feed rate to heat release rate.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV \quad [\text{kJ/h}]
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to SI power units (kW).**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{3600} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

**Step 3 — Equate total heat release to the product of grate area and design GHRR.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = A_{grate} \times q_{grate,\,design}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for grate area.**

$$
A_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{q_{grate,\,design}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{3600 \times q_{grate,\,design}} \quad [\text{m}^{2}]
$$

**Step 5 — Choose a rectangular layout.** Let $L_{grate}$ and $W_{grate}$ be length and width.

$$
A_{grate} = L_{grate} \times W_{grate}
$$

A typical aspect ratio: $\dfrac{L_{grate}}{W_{grate}} \approx 1.5 - 3.0$.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **[Worked Example 3.1]**
> A municipal incinerator must process **12 tonnes/hour** of MSW with an average **NCV of 9000 kJ/kg**. The design **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR) is 350 kW/m²**. Compute (a) the total heat release, (b) the required grate area, and (c) suitable dimensions if the length-to-width ratio is 2:1.

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{waste} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$
- $NCV = 9000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $q_{grate,\,design} = 350 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$
- $L_{grate} : W_{grate} = 2 : 1$

**(a) Total heat release:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \times 9000}{3600} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^{8}}{3600} = 30{,}000 \text{ kW} = 30 \text{ MW}
$$

**(b) Required grate area:**

$$
A_{grate} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{350} = 85.71 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**(c) Suitable dimensions:**

$$
L_{grate} \times W_{grate} = 85.71
$$

Let $W_{grate} = x$, then $L_{grate} = 2x$.

$$
2x^{2} = 85.71 \implies x^{2} = 42.86 \implies x = 6.55 \text{ m}
$$

Therefore:
$$
W_{grate} \approx 6.5 \text{ m}, \quad L_{grate} \approx 13.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Check GHRR:**

$$
q_{grate,\,actual} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{6.5 \times 13.1} = 352.3 \text{ kW/m}^{2} \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Combustion Air Requirement

**Step 1 — Ultimate analysis of MSW (typical Indian municipal waste).**

| Component | Mass Fraction |
|-----------|:-------------:|
| C (Carbon) | 25 % |
| H (Hydrogen) | 3 % |
| O (Oxygen) | 18 % |
| N (Nitrogen) | 0.5 % |
| S (Sulphur) | 0.2 % |
| Moisture | 40 % |
| Ash | 13.3 % |

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric oxygen demand per kg of dry combustible matter.**

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O \quad [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg combustible}]
$$

Where $\dfrac{8}{3}$ comes from the molar mass ratio $\dfrac{32 \times 2}{12} = \dfrac{8}{3}$ for $C + O_{2} \to CO_{2}$.

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air demand.**

$$
A_{stoich} = \dfrac{O_{2,\,stoich}}{0.232} \quad [\text{kg air / kg combustible}]
$$

The factor **0.232** is the mass fraction of oxygen in dry air (≈ 23.2 % by mass).

**Step 4 — Actual air with excess ratio $n$.**

$$
A_{actual} = n \times A_{stoich}, \quad n \approx 1.6 - 2.2 \text{ (typically 1.8)}
$$

**Numerical illustration for the MSW above (per kg of dry combustible):**

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}(0.25) + 8(0.03) + 0.002 - 0.18 = 0.6667 + 0.24 + 0.002 - 0.18 = 0.7287 \text{ kg O}_2
$$

$$
A_{stoich} = \dfrac{0.7287}{0.232} = 3.14 \text{ kg air / kg combust.}
$$

$$
A_{actual} = 1.8 \times 3.14 = 5.65 \text{ kg air / kg combust.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For Indian MSW (which has high moisture and ash), the air requirement on a *wet basis* is much higher — typically 4.5 – 6.5 kg air/kg waste.**

## 3.4 Residence Time and Forward Velocity

For an MSW grate:

$$
t_{res} = \dfrac{L_{grate}}{v_{forward}}
$$

For a reciprocating grate with stroke length $s$ and frequency $f$ (strokes/min):

$$
v_{forward} = \dfrac{s \times f}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}] \quad \text{or} \quad s \times f \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

**Numerical illustration:**

Given $s = 0.25 \text{ m}$, $f = 4 \text{ strokes/min}$, $L_{grate} = 13.1 \text{ m}$:

$$
v_{forward} = 0.25 \times 4 = 1.0 \text{ m/min} = 60 \text{ m/h}
$$

$$
t_{res} = \dfrac{13.1}{1.0} = 13.1 \text{ min}
$$

If residence is too short (< 10 min) → incomplete burnout, high LOI in ash. If too long (> 60 min) → low throughput.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a combustion grate. List its four primary functions. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A combustion grate is a refractory-lined, air-permeable platform on which MSW is progressively burned in a mass-burn incinerator, with primary combustion air supplied from below. Its four primary functions are:

1. **Transport** of waste from feed end to ash discharge end.
2. **Agitation** of the waste bed to expose fresh fuel surfaces.
3. **Distribution** of primary combustion air through the bed.
4. **Combustion zoning** for drying, pyrolysis, combustion, and burnout.

### Q2. Distinguish between stationary and moving grates. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stationary Grate | Moving Grate |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| Movement | None — manual poking | Mechanized reciprocation / rotation |
| Capacity | < 1 TPD (batch) | 1 – 1000 TPD (continuous) |
| Labour | High | Low |
| Combustion control | Poor | Excellent (zone-based air) |
| Suitability for MSW | Very limited | Industry standard |
| Examples | Old hospital waste units | Martin, Von Roll, Takuma |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the various types of combustion grates used in MSW incineration and explain the working principle of a **water-cooled reciprocating grate**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An MSW incinerator handles **8 TPH** of waste with NCV = **8500 kJ/kg**. The design **GHRR is 400 kW/m²**. Determine (i) total heat released, (ii) required grate area, and (iii) primary air requirement if the stoichiometric air demand is **4.5 kg air/kg waste** and excess air ratio is **1.8**, with primary air share **60 %**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Grate Classification and Working of Water-Cooled Reciprocating Grate**

**Classification (with brief explanation):**

1. **Stationary (Fixed) Grate** – No motion; used in small batch incinerators.
2. **Traveling Grate** – Continuous cast-iron belt; waste loaded at one end, ash discharged at the other.
3. **Reciprocating Grate** – Rows of bars move forward slowly and backward rapidly to advance and agitate waste. *Industry workhorse.*
4. **Rocking Grate** – Tilts cyclically; older design.
5. **Rotary Kiln** – Rotating refractory-lined cylinder; for hazardous waste.
6. **Fluidized Bed** – Sand bed suspended in air; for shredded waste.

**Working Principle of Water-Cooled Reciprocating Grate:**

- The grate consists of **cast-iron or alloy grate bars** arranged in 4–6 rows. Each row is mounted on a movable carrier driven by a **hydraulic cylinder** or eccentric crank.
- Each cycle has two phases:
  - **Forward stroke** (slow, ~ 10 s): The bar row pushes the waste bed forward by the stroke length (100 – 300 mm).
  - **Backward stroke** (fast, ~ 3 s): The bar row retracts; the waste, held by friction and ash bridges, remains in place, creating an **agitation effect**.
- The bars are **hollow**, with **pressurized water circulating at ~ 4 bar**, entering at ~ 40 °C and exiting at ~ 180 – 220 °C. The water absorbs heat, protecting the metal from slagging (ash melting) and from high-temperature corrosion.
- The heated water is **integrated into the boiler feedwater circuit** (cascade heating), improving overall plant efficiency by **3 – 6 %**.
- Underneath each row, a separate **air plenum** supplies **zone-controlled primary air**.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{waste} = 8 \text{ TPH} = 8000 \text{ kg/h}$
- $NCV = 8500 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $q_{grate,\,design} = 400 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$
- Stoichiometric air $= 4.5 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$
- Excess air ratio $n = 1.8$
- Primary air share $= 60\%$ of total air

**(i) Total heat released:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dfrac{8000 \times 8500}{3600} = \dfrac{6.8 \times 10^{7}}{3600} = 18{,}888.9 \text{ kW} \approx 18.89 \text{ MW}
$$

**(ii) Required grate area:**

$$
A_{grate} = \dfrac{18{,}888.9}{400} = 47.22 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**(iii) Primary air requirement:**

Total air per hour:

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\,actual} = n \times A_{stoich} \times \dot{m}_{waste} = 1.8 \times 4.5 \times 8000 = 64{,}800 \text{ kg/h}
$$

In volumetric terms (at STP, density ≈ 1.293 kg/Nm³):

$$
\dot{V}_{air,\,actual} = \dfrac{64{,}800}{1.293} = 50{,}116 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} \approx 50.12 \times 10^{3} \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

Primary air (60 %):

$$
\dot{V}_{primary} = 0.60 \times 50{,}116 = 30{,}070 \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

Secondary air (40 %):

$$
\dot{V}_{secondary} = 0.40 \times 50{,}116 = 20{,}046 \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **four combustion zones** that develop on a moving grate of an MSW incinerator. Discuss how **primary air distribution** is matched to each zone. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A municipal waste stream of **25,000 kg/h** has the following ultimate analysis: C = 22 %, H = 3 %, O = 16 %, N = 0.5 %, S = 0.2 %, moisture = 45 %, ash = 13.3 %. Calculate (i) stoichiometric air requirement in kg air/kg of dry combustible, and (ii) total actual air supplied if excess air ratio is **1.85**, with **65 % primary air** injected under the grate. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Four Combustion Zones on the Grate**

| Zone | Location | Process | Temperature (°C) | Primary Air % |
|:----:|:--------:|---------|:-----------------:|:------------:|
| **I — Drying** | First 25–30 % length | Moisture evaporation; endothermic | 200 – 600 | 25 – 30 |
| **II — Pyrolysis / Ignition** | 25 – 50 % | Volatiles released; char forms | 500 – 900 | 20 – 25 |
| **III — Combustion / Burnout** | 50 – 80 % | Char oxidation; peak exotherm | 900 – 1200 | 30 – 40 |
| **IV — Ash Cooling** | Last 20 % | Residual burnout; ash discharge | 600 – 800 | 10 – 15 |

**Air Distribution Matching:**

The **under-grate air plenum is divided into 3 – 4 compartments**, each connected to its own damper-controlled fan. The air split is matched to the local stoichiometric demand:

- **Drying zone**: High air demand (to carry away water vapour) but **low oxygen uptake** (combustion hasn't started). Air here is *pre-heated by the grate-cooling circuit* to assist drying.
- **Ignition zone**: Moderate air; primary role is to sustain devolatilization. Temperature rises sharply.
- **Combustion zone**: **Peak air supply** (40 % of total). Char oxidation is the main exothermic reaction.
- **Ash cooling zone**: Air here is *not for combustion* but to **quench the bottom ash**, preventing clinker formation and recovering sensible heat (pre-heated primary air).

> The combined outlet from all plenums is **hot primary air (180 – 250 °C)**, often used as combustion air or for in-bed drying.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Ultimate analysis (dry basis, normalizing 100 % combustible):**

Dry combustible = 100 % − 13.3 % ash = 86.7 % of total waste.
On dry-combustible basis:

- C = 22 / 0.867 = **25.37 %**
- H = 3 / 0.867 = **3.46 %**
- O = 16 / 0.867 = **18.45 %**
- N = 0.5 / 0.867 = **0.58 %**
- S = 0.2 / 0.867 = **0.23 %**

**(i) Stoichiometric oxygen (kg O₂ / kg dry combustible):**

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O
$$

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}(0.2537) + 8(0.0346) + 0.0023 - 0.1845
$$

$$
= 0.6765 + 0.2768 + 0.0023 - 0.1845 = 0.7711 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg d.c.}
$$

Stoichiometric air:

$$
A_{stoich} = \dfrac{0.7711}{0.232} = 3.324 \text{ kg air/kg dry combust.}
$$

**(ii) Total actual air supplied (on a wet waste basis):**

Dry combustible per kg wet waste = 0.867 kg.
Stoichiometric air per kg wet waste:

$$
A_{stoich,\,wet} = 0.867 \times 3.324 = 2.882 \text{ kg air/kg waste}
$$

Total actual air per hour (with $n = 1.85$):

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\,actual} = 1.85 \times 2.882 \times 25{,}000 = 133{,}242.5 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Primary air (65 %):

$$
\dot{m}_{primary} = 0.65 \times 133{,}242.5 = 86{,}608 \text{ kg/h}
$$

In volumetric terms (at STP):

$$
\dot{V}_{primary} = \dfrac{86{,}608}{1.293} \approx 66{,}980 \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h} \approx 66.98 \times 10^{3} \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT** mix up dry-combustible basis with wet-waste basis — convert carefully using $(1 - \text{ash fraction})$ and $(1 - \text{moisture fraction})$.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget that stoichiometric oxygen **subtracts the oxygen already present in the fuel** ($O$ term in the formula). Ignoring this gives an answer ~ 25 % too high.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse GHRR (heat per unit grate area) with **specific grate load** (mass per unit grate area per hour) — both have units but mean different things.
> 4. **Do NOT** use **gross calorific value (GCV)** in grate calculations without first converting to **NCV** (subtract latent heat of water vapour — including moisture and combustion-formed water).
> 5. **Do NOT** claim that primary air = 100 % of stoichiometric; in practice, **40 – 70 %** of total air is supplied as primary; the remainder is **secondary/over-fire air**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — "If you remember only 8 things before the exam, remember these:"**

- ☐ A **grate** is the moving combustion hearth of a mass-burn MSW incinerator, performing **transport, agitation, air distribution, zoning, and ash discharge**.
- ☐ The four sequential **combustion zones** are **Drying → Pyrolysis/Ignition → Combustion/Burnout → Ash Cooling**, each with controlled under-grate air share.
- ☐ **Water-cooled reciprocating grates** are the industry standard for MSW due to high temperature tolerance and recovery of sensible heat.
- ☐ The **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR)** formula is $q_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{3600 \times A_{grate}}$; typical design value **300 – 500 kW/m²**.
- ☐ Stoichiometric oxygen = $\dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$; divide by **0.232** to get stoichiometric air; multiply by **excess air ratio (1.6 – 2.2)** for actual air.
- ☐ **Primary air share = 40 – 70 %** of total air; injected below the grate.
- ☐ **Secondary / over-fire air = 30 – 60 %**; injected above the waste bed in the post-combustion zone to complete burnout of volatiles (target CO < 50 mg/Nm³).
- ☐ Key performance metrics: **residence time** (30 – 90 min), **specific grate load** (150 – 400 kg/m²·h), **burnout efficiency** (LOI of ash < 5 %), **grate fire temperature** (900 – 1200 °C peak).

> **End of Module 3 — Grates Notes**

## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pyrolysis** is a **thermochemical decomposition process** in which organic solid waste materials are heated to elevated temperatures (typically **300 °C – 900 °C**) in the **complete absence of oxygen** (or with a severely limited oxygen supply — sub-stoichiometric conditions), causing the long-chain hydrocarbon molecules in the waste to break down into smaller, more valuable products such as **pyrolytic char (solid residue)**, **pyrolysis oil / bio-oil (condensable liquids)**, and **non-condensable gases** (CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, and light hydrocarbons).

The word *Pyrolysis* is derived from the Greek words *pyro* (meaning **fire / heat**) and *lysis* (meaning **decomposition / breaking apart**) — literally translating to **"breaking apart by heat."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> Pyrolysis is classified as one of the **Thermochemical Conversion Technologies** for municipal solid waste (MSW), biomedical waste, plastic waste, and tyre waste — alongside **incineration**, **gasification**, and **torrefaction**. It is considered a **cleaner, low-emission alternative to open burning and landfilling** because it does not combust the waste directly and hence produces negligible NOₓ and dioxins when properly controlled.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Charcoal Kiln" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Cooking in a Sealed Pressure Cooker:**
> Imagine you are roasting coffee beans in a **completely sealed pressure cooker placed over a stove**. No air (oxygen) enters the cooker from outside. As the temperature rises, the beans do not *burn* with flames — instead, they **slowly darken, release aromatic vapors, and leave behind a black, carbon-rich solid (charcoal-like residue)**. The vapors that escape, when condensed, look like a dark brown liquid (similar to pyrolysis oil), while a portion escapes as combustible gases (similar to syngas).
>
> This is **exactly how pyrolysis works** — except instead of coffee beans, the "feed" is **mixed municipal solid waste, plastics, rubber tyres, or biomass**, and instead of a pressure cooker, an industrial-scale **reactor (pyrolyser)** is used.

### 1.3 Key Process Parameters (Critical for KTU)

- **Operating Temperature:** **300 °C to 900 °C** (Type-dependent)
- **Heating Rate:** **Slow: < 1 °C/s**, **Fast: 10 – 200 °C/s**, **Flash: > 1000 °C/s**
- **Residence Time:** **Seconds (flash)** to **hours (slow)**
- **Oxygen / Air Supply:** **Zero (pure pyrolysis)** or **< stoichiometric (sub-stoichiometric)**
- **Pressure:** Usually **atmospheric (~101.325 kPa)**; sometimes elevated
- **Particle Size of Feed:** **< 50 mm** for uniform heat transfer

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

- **MSW Feed Calorific Value:** **~ 8 – 12 MJ/kg**
- **Pyrolysis Oil (Bio-oil) Yield:** **30 – 70 wt%**
- **Char Yield:** **10 – 35 wt%**
- **Gas Yield:** **10 – 30 wt%**
- **Net Energy Recovery Efficiency:** **60 – 85 %** for modern systems
- **Operating Temperature Range Standard:** **500 °C – 800 °C** (most common industrial window)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry Behind Pyrolysis — Step-by-Step Logic

Pyrolysis is fundamentally a **depolymerisation + cracking + recombination** reaction sequence. The process can be broken down into the following sequential sub-steps:

- **Step 1 — Drying (Sensible Heat Phase, T < 200 °C):**
  Moisture and any surface-bound water on the waste are evaporated. This is an **endothermic** physical process; no chemical bonds are broken.

- **Step 2 — Devolatilisation / Primary Pyrolysis (200 °C – 500 °C):**
  Weak C–C and C–H bonds within cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin (and similar polymeric structures in plastics and rubber) begin to break. Volatile tars and condensable vapors are released. Char begins forming.

- **Step 3 — Secondary Reactions (Cracking & Reforming, 500 °C – 800 °C):**
  Primary pyrolysis vapors may further **crack into lighter gases** (H₂, CH₄, CO) if residence time is high or if catalysts are present. Secondary char may form via **repolymerisation (coking)** of heavy tars.

- **Step 4 — Char Carbonisation (T > 700 °C):**
  Residual carbonaceous matter is converted into a **carbon-rich solid char**, often used as a soil amendment, fuel briquette, or activated carbon precursor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Pyrolysis:** Without oxygen, the molecules cannot combust completely. Instead of generating CO₂ + H₂O (as in incineration), they undergo **reductive thermal fragmentation** — preserving much of the chemical energy in the form of **liquid fuels (bio-oil)** and **solid fuel (char)**. This is what makes pyrolysis attractive for **waste-to-energy** applications.

### 2.2 Classification of Pyrolysis

| **Type** | **Heating Rate** | **Residence Time (Solid)** | **Temperature (°C)** | **Dominant Product** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow Pyrolysis (Carbonisation)** | < 1 °C/s | Hours – Days | 300 – 500 | **Char (60 – 80 %)** |
| **Intermediate Pyrolysis** | 1 – 10 °C/s | Minutes | 500 – 650 | **Mixed (Oil + Char)** |
| **Fast Pyrolysis** | 10 – 200 °C/s | < 2 seconds (vapor) | 500 – 800 | **Bio-oil (60 – 75 %)** |
| **Flash Pyrolysis** | > 1000 °C/s | < 0.5 seconds | 800 – 1000 | **Bio-oil + Gas** |
| **Vacuum Pyrolysis** | Slow | Hours | 300 – 450 | **Bio-oil (low O content)** |

### 2.3 Product Spectrum & Engineering Utility

- **Pyrolytic Char:** Used as solid fuel, activated carbon precursor, soil conditioner (biochar), or carbon black substitute.
- **Pyrolysis Oil / Bio-oil:** Can be upgraded to transport fuels (via hydrodeoxygenation), or used directly in boilers and diesel engines.
- **Pyrolysis Gas (Pyrogas):** Rich in **CH₄, H₂, CO**; used to **provide process heat internally** (energy self-sufficiency) or in gas engines.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall Pyrolysis Reaction (Generic)** | $C_{x}H_{y}O_{z} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\;no\;O_{2}} Char \;+\; Bio\text{-}oil \;+\; Gas$ | Mass balance closure |
| **Mass Balance (Closure)** | $M_{feed} = M_{char} + M_{oil} + M_{gas} + M_{losses}$ | $M$ in kg; $\sum Y_{i} = 1$ |
| **Product Yield (Mass Fraction)** | $Y_{i} = \dfrac{m_{i}}{m_{feed}}$ | Dimensionless; i = char, oil, gas |
| **Energy Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{E_{char} + E_{oil} + E_{gas}}{E_{feed}}$ | Usually **60 – 85 %** |
| **Calorific Value of Feed (HHV)** | $HHV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | Modified Dulong formula (MJ/kg) |
| **Gas Composition by Volume** | $\sum V_{i} = V_{CO} + V_{CO_{2}} + V_{H_{2}} + V_{CH_{4}} + V_{C_{x}H_{y}}$ | Normalised to **100 %** |
| **Carbon Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{C_{char} + C_{oil} + C_{gas}}{C_{feed}}$ | Dimensionless (typically **0.70 – 0.95**) |
| **Stoichiometric Air Requirement** | $A_{st} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H + S - O$ | kg air per kg fuel (theoretical) |
| **Equivalence Ratio (for comparison)** | $ER = \dfrac{A_{actual}}{A_{stoichiometric}}$ | Pyrolysis: $ER \approx 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Pyrolysis is widely deployed for **plastic waste valorization** (especially polyolefins — PE, PP, PS), **end-of-life tyre recycling** (recovering carbon black + oil + steel), **biomass-to-bio-oil plants**, and **biomedical waste treatment** (since high-temperature anaerobic decomposition destroys pathogens effectively).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mass Balance Derivation for a Pyrolysis Reactor

Consider a **steady-state** pyrolysis reactor operating on a feed stream $F$ (kg/h) with products: **Char (C), Oil (O), Gas (G)** and negligible losses.

**Governing Conservation Equation (Mass):**

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas} + \dot{m}_{loss}$$

For an **ideal recovery assumption** where $\dot{m}_{loss} \approx 0$:

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}$$

**Dividing throughout by $\dot{m}_{feed}$ to obtain yield fractions:**

$$1 = Y_{char} + Y_{oil} + Y_{gas}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**

> **Given:** A pyrolysis plant processes **1000 kg/h** of shredded mixed plastic waste. The product yields (by mass) are:
> - Char: **15 %**
> - Oil: **55 %**
> - Gas: **25 %**
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Mass flow rate of each product. (b) Verify mass balance closure.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Compute each product flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{char} = 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \; kg/h$$

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.55 \times 1000 = 550 \; kg/h$$

$$\dot{m}_{gas} = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250 \; kg/h$$

Step (b) — Verify mass balance:

$$\dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas} = 150 + 550 + 250 = 950 \; kg/h$$

**Mass loss** $= 1000 - 950 = 50 \; kg/h$ (typically due to moisture loss, unaccounted particulates, and minor measurement errors).

**Closure** = $950/1000 = \mathbf{95\%}$ — acceptable for an industrial unit.

---

### 3.2 Energy Balance Derivation

For an **adiabatic, steady-state pyrolysis reactor**, the energy input (sensible heat + partial combustion of non-condensable gases) must balance the energy output (energy content of products + latent heat losses).

**Generic Energy Balance:**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} + \dot{m}_{feed}\,HHV_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char}\,HHV_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil}\,HHV_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}\,HHV_{gas} + \dot{Q}_{loss}$$

**Rearranging for Energy Recovery Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{char}\,HHV_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil}\,HHV_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}\,HHV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{feed}\,HHV_{feed}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> **Given:** Feed = 1000 kg/h MSW with $HHV_{feed} = 10\;MJ/kg$. Product HHV values: Char = 22 MJ/kg, Oil = 28 MJ/kg, Gas = 18 MJ/kg. Yields: 15 %, 55 %, 25 %.

**Step 1 — Energy in feed:**

$$E_{feed} = 1000 \times 10 = 10{,}000 \; MJ/h$$

**Step 2 — Energy in each product stream:**

$$E_{char} = 150 \times 22 = 3300 \; MJ/h$$

$$E_{oil} = 550 \times 28 = 15{,}400 \; MJ/h$$

$$E_{gas} = 250 \times 18 = 4500 \; MJ/h$$

**Step 3 — Total energy recovered:**

$$E_{recovered} = 3300 + 15{,}400 + 4500 = 23{,}200 \; MJ/h$$

**Step 4 — Recovery efficiency:**

$$\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{23{,}200}{10{,}000} = 2.32 = \mathbf{232\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A recovery efficiency of > 100 % is **physically possible** in pyrolysis because the **endothermic decomposition reaction absorbs heat during the process**, but the products — being in higher-energy chemical states (especially pyrolysis oil with many unsaturated hydrocarbons) — contain **more chemical energy per kg** than the original mixed waste. This is the *energy densification* advantage of pyrolysis over direct incineration.

---

### 3.3 Carbon Conversion Efficiency Derivation

Define the **Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE)**:

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{C_{char} + C_{oil} + C_{gas}}{C_{feed}}$$

Where $C_i$ represents the **mass of carbon** in product $i$.

**In terms of mass fractions and elemental compositions:**

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{Y_{char}\cdot\%C_{char} + Y_{oil}\cdot\%C_{oil} + Y_{gas}\cdot\%C_{gas}}{\%C_{feed}}$$

**Worked Example:**

> **Given:** Feed contains 50 % C. Char (15 % yield) = 80 % C; Oil (55 % yield) = 70 % C; Gas (25 % yield) contains carbon equivalent (CH₄ + CO) = 30 %.

**Numerator:**

$$(0.15)(0.80) + (0.55)(0.70) + (0.25)(0.30)$$

$$= 0.12 + 0.385 + 0.075 = 0.58$$

**Denominator** = $0.50$

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{0.58}{0.50} = \mathbf{1.16} \; (116\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CCE > 100 %?** This occurs because some **oxygen and hydrogen originally bound to carbon in the feedstock are released** during pyrolysis, leaving behind a **carbon-enriched** product. The numerator expresses the fraction of feed mass that ends up as carbon in the products, divided by the feed's original carbon fraction — so it can exceed 1.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Pyrolysis vs. Other Thermochemical Processes

| **Parameter** | **Pyrolysis** | **Incineration** | **Gasification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen Supply** | None / trace | Excess (ER > 1) | Sub-stoichiometric (ER 0.2 – 0.4) |
| **Temperature (°C)** | 300 – 900 | 850 – 1200 | 700 – 1200 |
| **Dominant Product** | Char + Oil + Gas | Heat + Ash | Syngas (CO + H₂) |
| **Air Emissions** | Very low NOₓ, no SO₂ | High NOₓ, CO₂, dioxins | Moderate |
| **Energy Recovery** | Oil + Char reuse | Heat-to-power | Gas-to-power |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow of a Typical Pyrolysis Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MSW Feedstock: Plastics, Tyres, Biomass] --> B[Shredder and Sieve Unit]
    B --> C[Drying Section: Moisture Removal at 110 to 150 C]
    C --> D[Feed Hopper with Screw Conveyor]
    D --> E[PYROLYSIS REACTOR 300 to 900 C Anaerobic]
    E --> F[Char Discharge and Cooling Screw]
    F --> F1[Char Storage and Bagging Unit]
    E --> G[Hot Vapor and Gas Stream]
    G --> H[Quench Condenser: Rapid Cooling]
    H --> H1[Pyrolysis Oil Collection Tank]
    H --> I[Non-Condensable Gas Scrubber]
    I --> I1[Gas Cleaning: Tar and Particulate Removal]
    I1 --> J[Non-Condensable Pyrogas: CH4 CO H2 CO2]
    J --> K[Internal Combustor for Process Heat Recycle]
    K --> E
    J --> L[Gas Engine or Boiler for Power Generation]
    F1 --> M[End Use: Soil Amendment, Solid Fuel, Activated Carbon]
    H1 --> N[End Use: Fuel Oil, Chemical Feedstock]
    L --> O[Electricity Export to Grid]
```

### 4.2 Pyrolysis Reactor Type Comparison — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fixed Bed Reactor: Slow Pyrolysis, Batch Mode]
    P2[Fluidised Bed Reactor: Fast Pyrolysis, Continuous]
    P3[Rotary Kiln Reactor: Continuous, Mixed Waste]
    P4[Auger Reactor: Continuous, Slow to Intermediate]
    P5[Plasma Pyrolysis: Very High T, Hazardous Waste]

    P1 --> R1[Char Dominant Output]
    P2 --> R2[Bio Oil Dominant Output]
    P3 --> R3[Balanced Tri Product Output]
    P4 --> R4[Char plus Oil Dominant Output]
    P5 --> R5[Syngas plus Vitrified Slag]
```

### 4.3 Mass Balance Flow (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Feed 1000 kg per hour MSW] --> R[Pyrolysis Reactor]
    R --> CH[Char Output 150 kg per hour]
    R --> OL[Pyrolysis Oil Output 550 kg per hour]
    R --> GA[Non Condensable Gas Output 250 kg per hour]
    R --> LS[Unaccounted Losses 50 kg per hour]
    CH --> OUT1[To Char Utilisation]
    OL --> OUT2[To Oil Storage and Refining]
    GA --> OUT3[To Gas Combustor for Process Heat]
```

---

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define pyrolysis. List any three operating parameters that influence the product distribution in a pyrolysis reactor.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Pyrolysis is the thermochemical decomposition of organic solid waste in the **absence of oxygen** (or with limited oxygen) at temperatures typically between **300 °C – 900 °C**, yielding three product fractions — **char, bio-oil (pyrolysis oil), and non-condensable gases**.
>
> **Three Operating Parameters (1 mark, any 3):**
> 1. **Reactor temperature** — Higher T favours gas yield; lower T favours char.
> 2. **Heating rate** — Slow heating gives char; fast heating gives oil.
> 3. **Vapour residence time** — Long residence promotes secondary cracking to gas.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **slow pyrolysis**, **fast pyrolysis**, and **flash pyrolysis** with respect to heating rate and dominant product.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Type** | **Heating Rate** | **Dominant Product** |
|---|---|---|
| Slow Pyrolysis | < 1 °C/s | Char (60 – 80 %) |
| Fast Pyrolysis | 10 – 200 °C/s | Bio-oil (60 – 75 %) |
| Flash Pyrolysis | > 1000 °C/s | Bio-oil + Gas |

(1 mark per correct row, **3 marks total**.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the working of a **fixed-bed pyrolysis reactor** for the treatment of **mixed plastic waste**. Discuss the role of each unit operation involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pyrolysis plant processes **2000 kg/day** of segregated plastic waste. The mass yields obtained are: Char = 12 %, Oil = 60 %, Gas = 22 %. The calorific values are: Feed = **18 MJ/kg**, Char = **26 MJ/kg**, Oil = **40 MJ/kg**, Gas = **20 MJ/kg**.
Calculate: **(i)** Daily mass of each product, **(ii)** Total daily energy recovery, **(iii)** Overall energy recovery efficiency. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Working of a Fixed-Bed Pyrolysis Reactor (7 marks):**

A **fixed-bed (or batch-type) pyrolysis reactor** is a vertical cylindrical vessel made of high-grade stainless steel, externally heated by electrical resistance heaters or by hot gas combustion.

- **Feed Preparation:** Segregated plastic waste is shredded to **< 50 mm** size and dried to reduce moisture below **5 %**.
- **Loading:** Waste is charged into the reactor through a top hatch which is then sealed **airtight** to maintain anaerobic conditions.
- **Heating Phase:** The bed temperature is raised gradually from ambient to **400 – 550 °C** over **2 – 6 hours**.
- **Vapor Generation:** Plastic polymers (PE, PP, PS) undergo **chain scission**, releasing long-chain hydrocarbon vapors and lighter gases.
- **Vapor Treatment:** Vapors are routed through a **condenser** where heavy fractions liquefy into pyrolysis oil; non-condensable gases are flared or recycled.
- **Residue Discharge:** Solid char is collected at the bottom after cooling under a **nitrogen blanket** to prevent auto-ignition.

**Role of Each Unit Operation:**
- Shredder — uniform particle size for **uniform heat transfer**.
- Dryer — moisture removal to **improve energy efficiency**.
- Reactor — controlled **anaerobic heating**.
- Condenser — **liquid fuel recovery**.
- Scrubber — **emission control**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Daily mass of each product:**

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = 2000 \; kg/day$$

$$\dot{m}_{char} = 0.12 \times 2000 = \mathbf{240 \; kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.60 \times 2000 = \mathbf{1200 \; kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{gas} = 0.22 \times 2000 = \mathbf{440 \; kg/day}$$

**Total accounted** $= 240 + 1200 + 440 = 1880 \; kg/day$
**Unaccounted loss** $= 2000 - 1880 = \mathbf{120 \; kg/day}$ (~6 %)

**(ii) Daily energy recovered:**

$$E_{char} = 240 \times 26 = 6240 \; MJ/day$$

$$E_{oil} = 1200 \times 40 = 48{,}000 \; MJ/day$$

$$E_{gas} = 440 \times 20 = 8800 \; MJ/day$$

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} = 6240 + 48{,}000 + 8800 = 63{,}040 \; MJ/day}$$

**(iii) Energy recovery efficiency:**

$$E_{feed} = 2000 \times 18 = 36{,}000 \; MJ/day$$

$$\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{63{,}040}{36{,}000} = \mathbf{1.751 = 175.1\%}$$

**Interpretation:** > 100 % indicates **chemical energy densification** during pyrolysis (typical of plastic-rich feedstock due to deoxygenation and hydrogen enrichment of the oil fraction).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Internal Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental advantages** of pyrolysis over **incineration** for municipal solid waste (MSW). Compare the emission profiles of both processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **overall mass balance equation** for a pyrolysis reactor and explain why **mass loss** is observed even in a well-operated system. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Pyrolysis vs. Incineration — Environmental Comparison (7 marks):**

| **Environmental Aspect** | **Incineration** | **Pyrolysis** |
|---|---|---|
| **NOₓ Emissions** | High (due to high T combustion) | Very low (anaerobic) |
| **SO₂ Emissions** | Moderate to high | Negligible |
| **Dioxins / Furans** | Possible (esp. PVC feed) | Negligible (no Cl₂ generation) |
| **Particulate Matter** | High | Low (char traps heavy metals) |
| **CO₂ / Greenhouse** | High (combustion) | Lower (carbon retained in char) |
| **Solid Residue** | Toxic bottom ash + fly ash | Non-leaching biochar |
| **By-Product Recovery** | Heat only | Oil + Gas + Char (value-added) |

(4 marks for comparison table; **3 marks** for explanation of dioxin avoidance mechanism and char's metal-immobilisation property.)

**(b) Mass Balance Derivation (7 marks):**

The steady-state mass balance over a control volume enclosing the pyrolysis reactor is:

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$

For a single-feed reactor:

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas} + \dot{m}_{loss}$$

**Normalising by feed mass** to get yield fractions:

$$1 = Y_{char} + Y_{oil} + Y_{gas} + Y_{loss}$$

**Sources of Mass Loss (3 marks):**
- **Moisture evaporation** — moisture in feedstock is not captured in any product stream.
- **Uncondensed light hydrocarbons** — vapors that escape as fugitive emissions.
- **Particulate carryover** — fine char dust lost with the gas stream.
- **Measurement uncertainty** — sampling and weighing errors at the plant scale.

A **typical acceptable closure** is **> 90 %** for an industrial unit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse pyrolysis with gasification** — Pyrolysis = **zero oxygen**; Gasification = **sub-stoichiometric oxygen**.
> 2. **Do NOT report energy efficiency > 100 % as "impossible"** — Explain the *chemical densification* concept (deletion of oxygen/hydrogen from feed releases energy-rich products).
> 3. **Always state the temperature range** (300 – 900 °C) explicitly — KTU examiners award marks for this specification.
> 4. **Avoid writing "combustion" when you mean "pyrolysis"** — mark deduction of 1 mark typically.
> 5. **Always include units (kg/h, MJ/kg, °C)** in numerical answers — **-0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Pyrolysis = **thermochemical decomposition of waste in absence of oxygen** at **300 – 900 °C**.
- Three product streams: **Char (solid)**, **Bio-oil (liquid)**, **Pyrogas (gaseous)**.
- **Slow pyrolysis** → high char yield. **Fast pyrolysis** → high bio-oil yield. **Flash pyrolysis** → gas + oil.
- Mass balance closure: $\sum Y_i = 1$ (with allowable loss < 10 %).
- Modified **Dulong formula** used for HHV estimation of feed.
- Pyrolysis is **cleaner than incineration** — no dioxins, low NOₓ, char immobilises heavy metals.
- **Energy recovery efficiency** can exceed 100 % due to **chemical densification** of products.
- **Reactor types:** Fixed bed, Fluidised bed, Rotary kiln, Auger, Plasma.
- **Suitable feedstocks:** MSW (after segregation), plastics (PE, PP, PS), tyres, biomass, biomedical waste.
- **ER ≈ 0** is the standard reference for pure pyrolysis.
- **Typical industrial operating window:** **500 – 800 °C** at **atmospheric pressure**.
- **Bio-oil is acidic and oxygenated** — requires **upgrading (hydrodeoxygenation)** for use as transport fuel.
- **KTU frequently tests:** (1) definitions, (2) process flow diagram, (3) numerical mass/energy balance, (4) comparison with incineration/gasification.

---

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility (TSDF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Hazardous Waste Context)**
> A **Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility (TSDF)** is an engineered, regulated, centralized installation authorized under the *Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016* (MoEFCC, India) for the safe handling of industrial hazardous wastes. It integrates three core functions:
> 1. **Treatment** – physical, chemical, biological, or thermal processes to reduce toxicity, volume, or mobility of waste.
> 2. **Storage** – temporary containment in engineered units (warehouses, tanks, containment areas) prior to treatment or disposal.
> 3. **Disposal** – permanent isolation of treated residue in a *Secure Landfill (SLF)* with composite liner and leachate collection systems.

### 🏥 Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital for Hazardous Waste"
Think of a TSDF exactly like a **multi-specialty hospital for sick (hazardous) materials**. 
- The **Emergency Ward** = Treatment units (e.g., incinerator, stabilization/solidification plant) where the waste is "cured" of its toxic nature.
- The **Pharmacy/Store Room** = Storage bays (where waste awaiting treatment or transportation is "quarantined").
- The **Cemetery/Cremation Ground** = Secured Landfill (where the final, non-recoverable residues are permanently interred under engineered barriers).
- The **regular medical check-ups** = Groundwater & gas monitoring wells surrounding the facility.

Just as a hospital cannot dump patients' remains in a public park, a TSDF ensures industrial waste never enters the open environment.

### 🧷 Key Operational Anchors
- **Operator Model:** In India, TSDFs are commonly operated by a **Common TSDF Operator** (a private/public entity) on behalf of multiple waste generators (the *Original Producer* / *Occupier* principle).
- **Site Selection Rule:** Must be **> 200 m** from a habitation, **> 200 m** from a highway, and avoid flood plains (Rule 6, HOWM Rules 2016).
- **Standard Permeability of Liner:** Clay liner permeability $\le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ **m/s** (≈ 1 Darcy limit reduced by factor 10⁶).
- **Monitoring Period:** **Post-closure monitoring for at least 30 years** is mandated for hazardous waste landfills (CPCB guidelines).

---

## 1.2 Integrated Waste Management (IWM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Integrated Waste Management (IWM)** is a sustainable, hierarchical, and holistic waste management strategy that combines multiple complementary techniques — **source reduction, reuse, recycling, composting/anaerobic digestion, incineration with energy recovery, and sanitary landfilling** — into a coordinated system. The objective is to *maximize resource recovery*, *minimize environmental burden*, and *optimize economic efficiency* by selecting the most appropriate technology at each stage of the waste stream, governed by the principle of the **Waste Management Hierarchy**.

### 🌱 Real-World Analogy — "The Balanced Diet for a City"
A city disposing 100 % of its waste into a landfill is like a person eating *only junk food*: cheap and easy in the short term, but disastrous long term. 
**IWM is the balanced diet:** 
- Vegetables & fruits 🥦 = **Source Reduction & Reuse** (prevention).
- Composting ♻️ = **Organic recycling** (turning waste biomass into soil).
- Incineration 🔥 = **Energy recovery** (burning non-recyclables to generate electricity — like eating protein for energy).
- Landfill ⛰️ = **Controlled disposal** of the *unavoidable* inert residue — like the inevitable small waste your body produces.

A dietitian (the **IWM planner**) balances these so that the *least possible* reaches the landfill.

### 📊 Quantitative Anchors for IWM
- **The Waste Hierarchy (descending priority):** **Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > Recover > Treat > Dispose** (5R / 6R concept).
- **Sustainable Target (EU & Indian Vision):** Achieve **< 10 % landfilling** and **> 70 % recycling/recovery** of municipal solid waste by 2030.
- **Energy from 1 tonne MSW via incineration:** Approximately **500–700 kWh** of electricity (gross).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IWM and TSDF are explicitly listed as *Module 4* outcomes in PECHT861. Questions frequently test the *interconnection* between the two — e.g., "How does IWM reduce the burden on a TSDF?" or "What role does a TSDF play in the residue layer of the IWM hierarchy?"

---

# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TSDF — Detailed Functional Anatomy

A modern TSDF is not a single structure but a **cluster of engineered units** operating under one Common Hazardous Waste Treatment, Storage and Disposal Facility (CHWTSDF) license.

### 2.1.1 Functional Sub-Units

| # | Sub-Unit | Engineering Function | Key Design Parameter |
|---|----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Secure Landfill (SLF)** | Permanent disposal of stabilized/treated residue | Composite liner: HDPE (1.5–2.5 mm) + clay (≥ 1 m, k ≤ 1×10⁻⁹ m/s) |
| 2 | **Leachate Collection & Treatment System (LCTS)** | Captures & treats contaminated liquid percolating through waste | Leachate head on liner ≤ 30 cm; HDPE perforated pipes (Ø 100–200 mm) |
| 3 | **Landfill Gas (LFG) Collection System** | Captures methane (CH₄) and CO₂ from anaerobic decomposition | Typical landfill gas: 40–60 % CH₄, 30–50 % CO₂, trace H₂S |
| 4 | **Stabilization/Solidification (S/S) Plant** | Binds contaminants into a low-permeability monolith | Compressive strength ≥ 0.35 MPa (USEPA TCLP limit) |
| 5 | **Incineration Unit** | Thermal destruction of organic hazardous waste | DRE (Destruction & Removal Efficiency) ≥ 99.99 % for principal organics |
| 6 | **Storage Bay (Hazardous Waste Warehouse)** | Temporary containment in bunded, segregated cells | Bund capacity ≥ 110 % of largest container |
| 7 | **Monitoring Well Network** | Long-term surveillance of groundwater | Minimum 4 wells (1 upstream + 3 downstream) |
| 8 | **Stormwater Management** | Prevents clean rainwater from becoming contaminated | Runoff coefficient C = 0.5–0.9 for paved industrial areas |

### 2.1.2 Site Selection — Sequential Logic
1. **Avoidance Test:** Reject sites in *eco-sensitive zones, flood plains, high water table* (< 1.5 m below base of landfill), or *seismically active zones* (Seismic Zone IV & V require special design).
2. **Distance Test:** Reject if within **200 m** of habitation, **200 m** of highway, **100 m** of river.
3. **Geological Test:** Verify the **insitu soil permeability ≤ 1×10⁻⁷ m/s** (clay/silty-clay preferred).
4. **Hydrogeological Test:** Annual groundwater flow direction, gradient, and aquifer classification must be mapped.
5. **Buffer & Capacity Test:** Confirm cell capacity (m³) ≥ 20 years of regional hazardous waste generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these parameters?** They form a *defense-in-depth* strategy: if the primary liner fails, the secondary clay liner catches the leak; if both fail, the monitoring wells detect contamination before it spreads.

---

## 2.2 Integrated Waste Management (IWM) — Hierarchy & Implementation

### 2.2.1 The 5R/6R Framework

$$\text{Integrated Hierarchy} = \text{Refuse} \rightarrow \text{Rethink} \rightarrow \text{Reduce} \rightarrow \text{Reuse} \rightarrow \text{Recycle} \rightarrow \text{Recover} \rightarrow \text{Residual Disposal}$$

For a typical Indian urban system, the IWM flow looks like:

| Stream | IWM Pathway | Technology | Typical % of MSW |
|--------|-------------|------------|------------------|
| **Organic / Wet Waste** (kitchen, garden) | Composting / Anaerobic Digestion / Biogas | Windrow composting, vermicomposting, N-SBR (Nieder-Schoeller Bioreactor) | 40–60 % |
| **Recyclable Dry Waste** (paper, plastic, metal, glass) | Material Recovery Facility (MRF) | Sorting, baling, shredding | 15–25 % |
| **Combustible Non-Recyclable** (multi-layer plastics, soiled paper) | Incineration with Energy Recovery (WtE) | Mass-burn / fluidized bed | 10–20 % |
| **Hazardous / E-Waste / Biomedical** | TSDF, Common E-Waste Dismantler, BMW Incinerator | Treatment + Secure Landfill | 1–3 % |
| **Inert / Construction & Demolition** | Inert Landfill / Reuse as fill | Crushing, screening | 10–30 % |
| **Unavoidable Residual** | Engineered Sanitary Landfill | Engineered cell with liner, gas capture | 5–15 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The question "*Discuss the role of TSDF within an IWM framework*" is essentially asking: *The TSDF occupies the very bottom of the IWM hierarchy* — it handles the **residual hazardous stream** that cannot be reduced, reused, recycled, or recovered. An effective IWM *shrinks* the volume reaching the TSDF, extending its operational life and reducing long-term liability.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Instructions for the Student:** Master the units and constant values in this table — they are the most-missed items in KTU numerical questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Landfill Volume (V)** | $V = \dfrac{M \times t \times C}{\rho_c}$ | M = annual waste input, t = design life, C = compacted waste factor (0.7–0.85), ρc = compacted density (400–700 kg/m³) | m³ |
| 2 | **Leachate Generation (Q_L)** | $Q_L = P \times A \times K$ | P = annual precipitation, A = landfill area, K = runoff/absorption coefficient (0.3–0.7) | m³/yr |
| 3 | **Soil Liner Permeability Limit** | $k \le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ **m/s** | Regulatory threshold for clay component of composite liner | m/s |
| 4 | **Darcy's Law (Leachate flow through liner)** | $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ | i = hydraulic gradient; for HDPE liner, $k_{HDPE} \le 1 \times 10^{-13}$ m/s | m³/s |
| 5 | **Mass of Leachate Contaminant** | $m_c = C \times Q_L \times t$ | C = contaminant concentration (mg/L); result in mg | mg |
| 6 | **Composting C/N Ratio (Optimal)** | $\dfrac{C}{N} \approx 25:1 \text{ to } 35:1$ | Ideal range for aerobic microbial activity | — |
| 7 | **Anaerobic Biogas Yield (BMP)** | $BMP \approx 0.4 \text{–} 0.5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}$ | Biochemical Methane Potential per kg of Volatile Solids destroyed | m³/kg |
| 8 | **Incinerator Mass Balance (Clausius)** | $m_{in} = m_{out} + m_{ash} + m_{stack}$ | Conservation of mass in WtE plant | kg |
| 9 | **Energy Recovery (WtE)** | $E = M \times CV \times \eta_{plant}$ | M = mass, CV = calorific value (~1000–2500 kcal/kg for MSW), η = 18–25 % net | kWh |
| 10 | **DRE (Destruction & Removal Efficiency)** | $DRE = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | $\ge 99.99$ % for principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs) | % |
| 11 | **TCE Reduction (3R model)** | $TCE = \dfrac{M_{landfill} \times 0.058}{10^6}$ | Equivalent methane emissions (Mg CO₂-eq per Mg waste landfilled, IPCC default) | Mg CO₂-eq |
| 12 | **Diversion Rate (IWM Metric)** | $\eta_d = \dfrac{M_{total} - M_{landfilled}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | Performance indicator of an IWM system | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 12 (Diversion Rate)** is *the* single most-asked IWM metric in KTU boards. Practice it carefully.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility & Engineering Significance

- **For Environmental Engineers:** TSDFs are the *last line of defense* against hazardous waste pollution, and their design directly determines the long-term liability of a region. A poorly designed TSDF can contaminate groundwater for centuries.
- **For Urban Planners:** IWM determines whether a city is *sustainable* (loop closed) or *unsustainable* (linear take-make-dispose). Smart IWM cuts landfill costs by **40–70 %**.
- **For Policy Makers:** IWM shifts the economic burden from *end-of-pipe treatment* to *upstream prevention*, which is 5–10× cheaper per ton of pollution avoided.
- **For Industry:** Industries pay **operating fees** to the Common TSDF (often ₹ 3,000–15,000/ton) instead of maintaining their own facility — a classic *economy of scale* application.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Landfill Volume Required for a Municipal Corporation

**Problem Context:** A city generates $M = 450$ tonnes/day of solid waste. The TSDF site is engineered for a design life of $t = 20$ years. The waste is compacted to a density $\rho_c = 600$ kg/m³ with a compaction factor $C = 0.80$ (i.e., 20 % volume reduction). Calculate the **required landfill volume**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert daily mass to annual mass.**

$$M_{annual} = M \times 365 = 450 \times 365 = 1{,}64{,}250 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total mass over design life.**

$$M_{total} = M_{annual} \times t = 1{,}64{,}250 \times 20 = 32{,}85{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert total mass to volume (uncompacted).**

$$V_{uncompacted} = \dfrac{M_{total}}{\rho_w}$$

We must use a representative uncompacted (loose) waste density. For a freshly delivered MSW (uncompacted), $\rho_w \approx 200 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (typical CPCB value). Therefore:

$$V_{uncompacted} = \dfrac{32{,}85{,}000}{200} = 16{,}425 \text{ m}^3$$

Wait — careful: this is much smaller than expected. We must apply the **compaction factor C = 0.80** in the *opposite* sense. The compaction factor indicates that after compaction, the volume is **0.80** of its original (i.e., 20 % reduction).

**Step 4 — Apply compaction factor to obtain required landfill volume.**

$$V_{landfill} = V_{uncompacted} \times C = 16{,}425 \times 0.80 = 13{,}140 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Convert to hectares-meters for practical sense (1 hectare = 10,000 m²).**

$$\text{If stack height} = h = 10 \text{ m, Area} = \dfrac{V}{h} = \dfrac{13{,}140}{10} = 1{,}314 \text{ m}^2 \approx 0.131 \text{ hectares}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{V_{landfill} = 13{,}140 \text{ m}^3 \quad (\text{or } 0.131 \text{ ha} \text{ at } 10 \text{ m stack height})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative KTU Presentation:** Many students may directly use $\rho_c = 600$ kg/m³ in the formula $V = \dfrac{M_{total}}{\rho_c}$ *and* apply the compaction factor again. **Do not double-compact.** Pick one route: either use the loose density + compaction factor, OR the compacted density directly *without* the factor. The KTU board will deduct marks for double-counting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Leachate Generation Estimate (Water Balance Method)

**Problem Context:** A TSDF has a top deck area $A = 25{,}000$ m². The regional annual precipitation $P = 1200$ mm/year. The runoff coefficient $C_r = 0.20$ (since most precipitation infiltrates or evaporates from a closed landfill cell). The absorption/absorption capacity is $K = 0.4$ (i.e., 40 % of incident precipitation becomes leachate). Compute **annual leachate volume**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert precipitation to meters.**

$$P = 1200 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \text{ m/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total precipitation volume over the cell.**

$$V_P = A \times P = 25{,}000 \times 1.2 = 30{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract surface runoff and evaporative loss.**

$$Q_{leachate} = V_P \times K = 30{,}000 \times 0.4 = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Express in litres per day (for treatment plant design).**

$$Q_{leachate, daily} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{365} \times 1000 = 32{,}876.7 \text{ L/day} \approx 32.88 \text{ kL/day}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{Q_L = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr} = 32.88 \text{ kL/day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick:** The coefficient K in the formula $Q_L = P \cdot A \cdot K$ is *not* a runoff coefficient — it is a leachate yield factor (sometimes called the *infiltration coefficient*). The two are reciprocally related. Always read the question stem for the exact definition.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diversion Rate Calculation under an IWM Plan

**Problem Context:** A municipality produces $M_{total} = 1000$ tonnes/day of municipal solid waste. Under the new IWM plan:
- **Composting** recovers $M_c = 280$ t/d
- **Recycling (MRF)** recovers $M_r = 220$ t/d
- **Incineration (WtE)** handles $M_w = 150$ t/d
- **Remaining** goes to engineered sanitary landfill.

Compute the **Diversion Rate** of the system.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute mass sent to landfill.**

$$M_{landfilled} = M_{total} - (M_c + M_r + M_w) = 1000 - (280 + 220 + 150) = 1000 - 650 = 350 \text{ t/d}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Diversion Rate formula.**

$$\eta_d = \dfrac{M_{total} - M_{landfilled}}{M_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1000 - 350}{1000} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Final evaluation.**

$$\eta_d = \dfrac{650}{1000} \times 100 = 65 \text{ \%}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\eta_d = 65 \text{ \%}}$$

This means the IWM system has **diverted 65 % of waste from landfill** — a good performance benchmark (CPCB recommends ≥ 75 % for sustainable cities).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** A frequent mistake is to *not* subtract the WtE mass from the landfill component. Note: WtE *is* considered a diversion (since it recovers energy instead of landfilling). Hence, $M_w = 150$ is added to the diversion pool. If a student excludes it, the diversion rate falls to **50 %** — a 15-point error, easily losing 2 marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of IWM Components

| IWM Component | Reduction in Volume Reaching TSDF | Environmental Risk | Capital Cost | Operational Skill |
|---------------|----------------------------------|--------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Source Reduction** | 5–15 % | Very Low | Very Low | Public Awareness |
| **Composting (Aerobic)** | 25–40 % (for organic fraction) | Low (Odor, leachate) | Low | Moderate |
| **Anaerobic Digestion** | 25–40 % + Energy Recovery | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High | High |
| **MRF / Material Recycling** | 15–25 % | Low | Low–Moderate | Moderate |
| **Incineration / WtE** | 60–80 % (volume) | High (Air emissions) | Very High | High |
| **Sanitary Landfill** | 0 % (final sink) | Moderate (Long-term) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Secured Landfill (TSDF)** | N/A (Receives only hazardous residue) | Low (if engineered) | High | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** No single technology is the *silver bullet*. **The strength of IWM lies in combining these technologies so that the *residual* reaching the TSDF/SLF is minimized, stable, and inert.** The matrix is a popular KTU 2024 exam comparison question.

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a TSDF

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InGen[Hazardous Waste Generators - Industries] --> TransP[Transporter - Authorized GPS-tracked Vehicle]
    TransP --> RecvBay[Receiving and Inspection Bay]
    RecvBay --> CharLab[Characterization Lab - TCLP Testing]
    CharLab --> StBay[Storage Bay - Segregated Bunded Cells]
    CharLab --> SStab[Stabilization and Solidification Plant]
    CharLab --> Incin[Incinerator - 850 to 1200 degC]
    StBay --> SStab
    StBay --> Incin
    SStab --> SLF[Secured Landfill - Composite Liner HDPE plus Clay]
    Incin --> AshMon[Ash Treatment and Metal Recovery]
    AshMon --> SLF
    SLF --> LCM[Leachate Collection Manifold]
    LCM --> LTP[Leachate Treatment Plant - RO plus MBR]
    SLF --> GasColl[Landfill Gas Collection Wells]
    GasColl --> GasUtil[Gas Engine or Flare Stack]
    LTP --> MonitorW[Groundwater Monitoring Wells - 4 to 8 nos]
    GasUtil --> MonitorW
    MonitorW --> Report[Quarterly Compliance Report to SPCB and CPCB]
    classDef safe fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#1f6f1f,color:#0e2a0e
    classDef risk fill:#ffe2c0,stroke:#a04a00,color:#3d1c00
    classDef info fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#155a99,color:#082747
    class SLF,LTP,MonitorW,GasUtil safe
    class Incin,SStab risk
    class Report,InGen,TransP info
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- *Green blocks* (SLF, LTP, MonitorW, GasUtil) represent **defense-in-depth** layers that contain and monitor contamination.
- *Orange blocks* (Incin, SStab) are **active treatment units** that destroy or stabilize hazards.
- *Blue blocks* are **logistical and compliance** elements — often undervalued in textbook diagrams but crucial in KTU's "discuss management structure" questions.

---

## 4.2 Integrated Waste Management (IWM) Hierarchy & Interconnection with TSDF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source - 100 percent Municipal or Industrial Waste] --> B[IWM Strategy]
    B --> L1[Tier 1 - Refuse and Reduce]
    B --> L2[Tier 2 - Reuse]
    B --> L3[Tier 3 - Recycle - MRF]
    B --> L4[Tier 4 - Recovery - Composting, AD, WtE]
    B --> L5[Tier 5 - Residual Disposal]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L3 --> MRF[Material Recovery Facility - Paper Plastic Metal Glass]
    L4 --> Comp[Aerobic Composting]
    L4 --> AD[Anaerobic Digestion - Biogas]
    L4 --> WtE[Waste to Energy Incineration]
    L5 --> SLF[Engineered Sanitary Landfill - MSW Residue]
    L5 --> TSDFH[TSDF - Hazardous Waste Residue Only]
    MRF --> Industry[Sent to Industry as Secondary Raw Material]
    Comp --> SoilAgri[Sent to Agriculture as Soil Conditioner]
    AD --> BiogasPlant[Biogas for Cooking or Power]
    WtE --> PowerGrid[Electricity Exported to Grid]
    classDef top fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600,color:#3d2e00
    classDef process fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#1f6f1f,color:#0e2a0e
    classDef sink fill:#ffd4d4,stroke:#991f1f,color:#3d0a0a
    class A,B,L1,L2,L3,L4,L5 top
    class MRF,Comp,AD,WtE,Industry,SoilAgri,BiogasPlant,PowerGrid process
    class SLF,TSDFH sink
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **IWM cascade** (top yellow blocks) shows the hierarchical philosophy — top tiers are preferred, lower tiers are only *fallbacks*.
- The **green blocks** are active resource-recovery nodes.
- The **red blocks** (SLF and TSDF) are the *final disposal endpoints* that receive only the inert or treated residue.
- **TSDF sits at the very bottom** of the IWM hierarchy for hazardous waste, while the **Sanitary Landfill** is the bottom for MSW residue.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Inside the Secured Landfill Cell (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Surface[Top Surface - Daily Soil Cover 15 cm plus Final Cover Vegetative] --> InfLayer[Intermediate Waste Lift - 2.5 to 5 m per day]
    InfLayer --> BotLayer[Bottom Waste Layer - Oldest Waste]
    BotLayer --> ClayL[Secondary Clay Liner - k le 1e-9 m per s]
    ClayL --> HDPEBot[Primary HDPE Geomembrane - 1.5 to 2.5 mm]
    HDPEBot --> LCS[Leachate Collection Layer - Gravel plus Perforated Pipe]
    LCS --> LCSump[Leachate Sump and Pump Station]
    LCSump --> LRO[Leachate Treatment - RO plus MBR]
    InfLayer --> GasWell[Vertical Gas Extraction Well - 50 to 100 mm dia]
    GasWell --> GasCollMan[Gas Collection Manifold - HDPE]
    GasCollMan --> GasEng[Gas Engine - 1 MW per 100000 tonnes waste]
    GasEng --> Flare[Backup Flare Stack - Auto-ignition at 700 degC]
    LCSump --> MonWell[Groundwater Monitoring Wells - 1 Up 3 Down]
    classDef layer fill:#fff0d4,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#3a2b00
    classDef barrier fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#155a99,color:#082747
    classDef monitor fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#1f6f1f,color:#0e2a0e
    class Surface,InfLayer,BotLayer layer
    class ClayL,HDPEBot,LCS,LCSump barrier
    class GasWell,GasCollMan,GasEng,Flare,MonWell,LRO monitor
```

**Engineering Logic:** The diagram shows a *top-down flow* of leachate (percolating through waste → collected at bottom) and *bottom-up flow* of gas (rising through waste → extracted at top). Both are intercepted by engineered barriers before reaching the environment.
